analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Jiri Prochazka -- Style & Profile

- **Data Tier: MEDIUM** (5+ UFC fights, full splits available, but last-3 SLpM 0.92–2.46 vs. career 5.69 is a massive anomaly requiring investigation — likely reflects fight-specific pace, not decay). **Archetype: Power Striker / Brawler. Tag: FORWARD.**
- **CHIN CRACKED alert (2 of 5 criteria met):** 2 KO losses in last 3 years (Pereira R2 twice — Apr 2024: 4:08, Jun 2024: 0:13), with the most recent being a R2 stoppage at 0:13 — extremely early, suggesting the chin absorbed damage in the first Pereira fight and cracked on re-exposure. KO'd by the same elite-level finisher twice in succession.
- **Defensive decay confirmed (Section 3.3):** Career SApM 5.60, strike defense 45%. These are already alarming baseline numbers. His style is inherently trade-heavy — he invites contact. Against a clean striker with 51% defense and 6.54 SLpM career pace, his willingness to eat shots is the core liability.
- **Win pattern is entirely striking-based:** 5 KO/TKO in last 8, 0 decisions, 1 sub (Teixeira, deep in R5 of a grind). He does not win by controlling pace or outworking — he wins by producing chaos and landing something clean. The Rountree and Hill wins (both R3 KOs) show he can find the finish late, but both opponents were also decelerating strikers, not volume threats.
- **Last-3 SLpM split (0.92, 2.46, 1.34):** All three numbers are dramatically below his career 5.69 average. This is not a fighter throwing at career pace — he has slowed considerably in recent bouts. Whether this reflects a conscious style shift (more patient), post-KO gun-shyness, or matchup-specific caution, the result is the same: he is not the volume monster the career line suggests. Label this **FLAT/DOWN on volume.** Per Rule 3.1, SLpM last-3 avg ≈ 1.57 vs. career 5.69 — **TRENDING DOWN (-72%). HIGH SIGNAL given opponent quality (Hill, Pereira ranked).**
- **Intangibles: Tapology data unavailable.** Layoff: 185 days (standard turnaround, no penalty per Rule 9.2). Age 33 in LHW — approaching cliff (36 for MW–LHW per Rule 9.7), no penalty yet but noted.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Carlos Ulberg -- Style & Profile

- **Data Tier: MEDIUM** (7 UFC fights, full splits available, but only 9-fight UFC sample and career averages may be skewed by early-career finishes against lower-level opposition). **Archetype: Volume Striker / Counter-Striker hybrid. Tag: BACKWARD / FORWARD depending on opponent — defaults to counter-based movement then opens up when hurt or dominant.** Career SLpM 6.54 vs. last-3 avg ≈ 1.69 — also dramatically below career pace, mirroring the same low-activity pattern seen in Prochazka's splits. Both fighters appear to operate at very low strike rates in recent bouts.
- **Defensive profile is the clearest edge:** Career SApM 4.04 (vs. Prochazka's 5.60), strike defense 51% (vs. Prochazka's 45%). Ulberg absorbs 28% fewer strikes per minute than Prochazka at the career level. TDD 85% (vs. Prochazka's 68%) — takedown defense is elite, neutralizing any grappling scrambles.
- **Last-3 strike accuracy trending DOWN:** Career 55% accuracy, last-3 avg ≈ 50.5% (64.7% vs. Reyes — highest of the three, but Reyes is a shot-for-shot target; 46.9% vs. Blachowicz; 40% vs. Oezdemir). The Oezdemir and Blachowicz decision wins were uninspiring volume-light performances. Flag: **Str.Acc TRENDING DOWN** from career baseline. Against a fighter like Prochazka who makes exchanges unpredictable, accuracy may drop further.
- **Finishing pattern skewed toward early rounds vs. lower opposition:** 5 KO/TKO wins, 4 of them in R1 (Menifield, Potieria, Negumereanu, Nchukwu, Reyes). The quality step-up against Blachowicz and Oezdemir produced decisions, not finishes. One KO loss (Nzechukwu, R2) — single data point, no CHIN CRACKED flag warranted, but noted.
- **Age: 35** — at the LHW cliff (36 per Rule 9.7 for MW–LHW). One year below the mechanical penalty threshold; no formal deduction, but listed as a monitoring flag. Layoff: 192 days — standard, no penalty.
- **Reach disadvantage: 77" vs. Prochazka's 80"** — Prochazka holds a 3" reach advantage. Per Rule 5.3, this is below the 4" threshold for a material edge flag, but it does mean Ulberg cannot simply stand on the outside and pot-shot — he must enter Prochazka's range to land cleanly.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

**Structural read (Section 5.1 matrix):** Both fighters are classified as Power/Volume Strikers. Per the matrix (Volume vs. Volume / Power vs. Power): cardio wins in volume-volume matchups; "whoever lands first" in power-power matchups. Given both fighters' recent last-3 data showing extremely suppressed output (Prochazka avg 1.57 SLpM last-3, Ulberg avg 1.69 SLpM last-3), this is not going to be a high-volume brawl — it will be a slow, low-activity striking chess match where the first clean, meaningful shot carries outsized weight. The structural question becomes: who is more accurate and more defensively sound when the exchanges are sparse? Answer: **Ulberg holds the defensive edge** (51% str.def vs. 45%, 4.04 SApM absorbed vs. 5.60). That gap matters enormously in a low-volume fight — every shot landed is a larger proportion of total offense.

**The CHIN CRACKED variable overrides the archetype matchup here.** Prochazka has been finished twice in two years by the same opponent, the second time in 0:13 of R2 — a catastrophic early stoppage signaling that his chin absorbs accumulation faster than his engine can tolerate. Ulberg is a legitimate knockout finisher (5 KOs in last 10, multiple R1 stoppages). Per Section 6.2: Prochazka meets criteria 1 (2+ KO losses in last 3 years) and criteria 2 (most recent KO came in R2 at 0:13 — effectively R1 pace). The chin flag is active and Ulberg is the power threat on the opposite side of it. When CHIN CRACKED meets a Power Striker at a line shorter than -150, this is flagged as a value fade on the cracked fighter (Rule 6.2). Prochazka is currently priced at +250, which means the market already knows this — but the question is whether +250 correctly represents his true win probability or is still offering value.

**Path to victory — Prochazka:** His realistic path is the same as every Prochazka win: he absorbs early damage, survives, and catches a tired or over-committed opponent late (Rountree R3, Hill R3, Teixeira R5). He is demonstrably a late-fight finisher against non-elite strikers. If Ulberg does not close the show early, Prochazka's unorthodox footwork and angles can generate a single clean counter in R2–R3. The 3" reach advantage also gives him slightly more lateral threat at distance. **Path to victory — Ulberg:** Apply early pressure, use the defensive gap (51% vs. 45% strike defense, 4.04 vs. 5.60 SApM), force Prochazka to reach into his range with the reach disadvantage, and land the cleaner, harder shot first. Prochazka's history shows he does not survive being walked down by a confident power striker early — and Ulberg has the TD defense (85%) to keep this standing if Prochazka somehow attempts a desperation grapple. Net intangible delta: minimal — both fighters have ~185–192 day layoffs, no camp change data, no weight miss data. The intangible field is essentially neutral; the edge lives in the stylistic and durability data.
analysis_sections.endings:
## 3-5 Most Likely Fight Endings (Ranked by Probability)

**Section 8 math — KO adjustment for Ulberg finishing Prochazka:**
- base_KO_rate (Ulberg): 5 KO wins / ~10 UFC-relevant fights ≈ 0.50
- opponent_str_def (Prochazka): 45% → 0.45
- chin_modifier: 1.4 (CHIN CRACKED)
- cardio_modifier: 1.0 (scheduled rounds unknown; treating as 3-round)
- adjusted_KO_prob = 0.50 × (1 − 0.45) × 1.4 × 1.0 = 0.50 × 0.55 × 1.4 = **0.385 (~39%)**

**Section 8 math — KO adjustment for Prochazka finishing Ulberg:**
- base_KO_rate (Prochazka): 5 KO/8 recent = ~0.625 career-adjusted; vs. ranked opponents (Hill, Rountree, Rakic): 3/3 = 0.75 — but also 0/2 vs. Pereira, so use 3/5 = 0.60
- opponent_str_def (Ulberg): 51% → 0.51
- chin_modifier: 1.0 (1 KO loss, 4+ years ago, no CHIN CRACKED flag)
- cardio_modifier: 1.0
- adjusted_KO_prob = 0.60 × (1 − 0.51) × 1.0 × 1.0 = 0.60 × 0.49 = **0.294 (~29%)**

**Decision probability (Section 8.4):** 1 − 0.385 − 0.294 − sub_prob_Ulberg (~0.01) − sub_prob_Prochazka (~0.02) ≈ **~0.30 (30%)**

---

**#1 — Carlos Ulberg wins by KO/TKO, Rounds 1–2**
Probability: 35%
Why: The CHIN CRACKED flag on Prochazka (two R2 KO losses in 24 months, the second in 0:13) combined with Ulberg's 85% TDD neutralizing any grappling diversions creates a clean path for an early finish. Ulberg's demonstrated finishing pace against standing opponents (Reyes R1 in 4:27, Menifield R1 in 0:12) and his superior defensive numbers (51% str.def, 4.04 SApM) mean he can absorb Prochazka's opening chaos and land the cleaner shot before the fight deepens. Prochazka has not survived a confident early-round striker pressuring him in recent memory.

**#2 — Carlos Ulberg wins by unanimous decision**
Probability: 22%
Why: Both fighters are operating at suppressed output (last-3 SLpM under 2.5 each), making a slow, measured chess match the realistic base case if neither lands a defining shot early. Ulberg's superior strike defense (51% vs. 45%) and lower absorption rate (4.04 vs. 5.60 SApM) mean he wins the volume-and-accuracy war on the cards even without a finish. His three-fight decision run (Blachowicz, Oezdemir, now potentially Prochazka if the finish never materializes) fits this pattern.

**#3 — Jiri Prochazka wins by KO/TKO, Round 2–3**
Probability: 25%
Why: Prochazka's last two wins followed an identical pattern — he absorbed damage early, appeared in trouble, then produced a counter-striking finish late (Rountree R3 3:04, Hill R3 3:01). If Ulberg fails to close before R2, Prochazka's unorthodox angles and willingness to brawl at close range can generate the single clean shot he needs. The 3" reach advantage provides slightly more threat at distance, and Ulberg's own chin history (1 KO loss, Nzechukwu R2) suggests he is not immune to late-fight power.

**#4 — Carlos Ulberg wins by KO/TKO, Round 3**
Probability: 8%
Why: Lower probability but non-trivial: if Prochazka survives early rounds and the fight grinds into R3, Ulberg's superior cardio baseline (lower SApM = less damage accumulating) positions him to finish a worn-down Prochazka who may be running on fumes after trading early. Prochazka has never won a decision in his UFC career — if he hasn't finished by R3, he typically has nothing left but survival mode.

*(Probabilities: 35 + 22 + 25 + 8 = 90%; remaining 10% covers miscellaneous — submission by Prochazka, doctor stoppage, or split-decision outcomes.)*
analysis_sections.betting:
## Betting Recommendation

**1. TIER LABEL: PLAY**

**2. PRIMARY PICK: Carlos Ulberg — Confidence: 62%**

**3. Edge Breakdown:**
- Ulberg current line: −300
- Implied probability (−300): 300 / 400 = **75.0%**
- De-vig: Prochazka +250 → 100/350 = 28.6%. Sum = 75.0 + 28.6 = 103.6%. True prob Ulberg = 75.0 / 103.6 = **72.4%**. True prob Prochazka = 28.6 / 103.6 = **27.6%.**
- Assessed probability (Ulberg): **62%** (35% KO win R1–2 + 22% decision + 8% late KO, stacked from Section 8 math — total Ulberg outcomes = 65%, conservatively adjusted to 62% given MEDIUM data tier cap)
- **Edge: 62% − 72.4% = −10.4%**

⚠️ **EDGE IS NEGATIVE.** The market has Ulberg priced correctly — or more precisely, it has him overpriced. At −300, Ulberg's implied true probability (72.4%) is HIGHER than my assessed probability (62%). There is no value on Ulberg at this number.

**Re-checking Prochazka (+250):**
- True market prob for Prochazka: **27.6%**
- Assessed probability for Prochazka: outcomes 3+4 = 25% + some decision Prochazka scenarios. Total Prochazka win probability from the ENDINGS math: ~35% (25% KO + 10% miscellaneous/decision)
- Assessed prob Prochazka: **35%**
- Edge on Prochazka: 35% − 27.6% = **+7.4%**

**PRIMARY PICK CORRECTED (Rule 13.8 — Fade Alert):**
> *"Initial read: Ulberg. After de-vig and probability assessment: Prochazka at +250 has +7.4% edge. Switching recommendation to Prochazka."*

**REVISED PRIMARY PICK: Jiri Prochazka (+250) — Confidence: 60%**

**Revised Edge Breakdown:**
- Line: Prochazka +250
- Implied prob: 28.6%
- De-vigged true prob: 27.6%
- Assessed prob: 35%
- Edge: **+7.4%** ✅ (above the 5% PLAY threshold)

**4. Stake Calculation (Quarter Kelly):**
- Decimal odds for +250: (250/100) + 1 = 3.50
- Kelly fraction = (0.074 × 3.50 − (1 − 0.35)) / (3.50 − 1) = (0.259 − 0.65) / 2.50 = −0.391 / 2.50

⚠️ **Kelly fraction is NEGATIVE.** This means the bet is mathematically −EV at the assessed probability. Quarter Kelly does not override a negative Kelly result — when Kelly is negative, the bet has no correct stake size. The model assessed 35% win probability; for a +250 play to be Kelly-positive, you need assessed probability above (1 / 3.50) = **28.6%**, which is satisfied (35% > 28.6%). Let me recheck:

- Kelly = (b × p − q) / b where b = net odds decimal (2.50), p = 0.35, q = 0.65
- Kelly = (2.50 × 0.35 − 0.65) / 2.50 = (0.875 − 0.65) / 2.50 = 0.225 / 2.50 = **0.09 (9%)**
- Quarter Kelly = 0.09 × 0.25 = 0.0225 → **0.2 units** (rounded to one decimal)

*(Note: The full Kelly formula in Section 11.4 uses decimal_odds as the full return including stake, not net — recalculating correctly gives a small but positive Kelly fraction.)*

**Recommended stake: 0.2 units**

**5. Target Line:** For +3% edge minimum on Prochazka, market true prob must be < 32%. That requires Prochazka to be no worse than approximately **+215** (at which point de-vigged true prob ≈ ~30%). **Target: Prochazka at +215 or better. Current +250 clears this with margin.**

**6. Data Tiers:**
- Jiri Prochazka: **MEDIUM** (5+ UFC fights, full splits, recent activity, CHIN CRACKED flag active)
- Carlos Ulberg: **MEDIUM** (full UFC splits, recent activity, no critical missing stats)
- Confidence cap for MEDIUM/MEDIUM: **67% maximum** — current assessed confidence of 60% is within bounds.

**7. Market Read:**
- Prochazka: Open +250 → Current +250 — **FLAT. No movement.**
- Ulberg: Open +250 → Current −300 — **550-cent move toward Ulberg. Massive sharp action on Ulberg.** This is not public money — public doesn't move a line from +250 to −300 unilaterally. This is a sharp/syndicate move.
- RLM: NO — no reverse line movement flag. Line moved consistently toward Ulberg.
- The market read **disagrees with the Prochazka value pick.** Per Rule 10.11: "Stat-read disagrees with the market → −5% confidence penalty." Confidence adjusted: 60% → **55%** (per Rule 10.11 and Rule 13.5 — stat-read vs. confirmed large market move → cap at 55%).
- Per Rule 10.4: "If your stat-based read disagrees with the RLM, re-examine your read first." This is not technically RLM (line moved with, not against, the public direction), but the magnitude of the move (550 cents) functions as a sharp signal. **The edge identified may be illusory** — the market has dramatically re-priced Ulberg, and the movement magnitude suggests informed money with knowledge the public stats don't capture (camp quality, sparring reports, Prochazka's physical condition post-two-KO-losses).

**REVISED TIER: LEAN (not PLAY)** — Confidence drops to 55% after applying the −5% market-disagreement penalty. Per Rule 13.2: 55–60% = LEAN. Per Rule 13.7: a PLAY requires ≥60% assessed prob AND ≥5% edge. The confidence is now at 55%, falling below the PLAY threshold.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION: LEAN — Jiri Prochazka (+250). NOT a full play. Small speculative position only at current line.**
- Confidence: **55%**
- Edge on paper: +7.4% (but confidence cap fires due to large market movement against the pick)
- Stake: **0 units formally (LEAN, not PLAY). If taking the speculative position: maximum 0.5 units.** Do not exceed.
- Target: +215 or better

**8. Hedge Verdict:**
- Best available: Ulberg −300 (FanDuel/DraftKings approximate), Prochazka +250
- Combined implied: 75.0% + 28.6% = **103.6%** → **NO ARB.** Book margin is 3.6%. No hedging structure produces profit. Take the value pick only.
- **VERDICT: NO ARB.**

**9. Props Worth Considering:**
- **"Fight goes to decision" — YES:** Section 8.4 calculated decision probability at ~30%. If this prop is available at implied probability below 30% (e.g., YES at +200 = 33.3% implied, or de-vigged below 30%), there is marginal value. Both fighters' last-3 SLpM data (Prochazka avg 1.57, Ulberg avg 1.69) confirm suppressed output — in a low-pace brawl, finishing becomes less likely. *Check props lines at your book; value exists only if the YES line is +200 or longer.*
- **"Fight ends in Round 1 — NO":** Both fighters' most recent finishes came in R1 (Ulberg over Reyes, 4:27) and R3 (Prochazka over Rountree, 3:04). Prochazka has never been finished in R1 in UFC. R1 KO probability is lower than the market typically assigns. *If this NO is available at reasonable vig, it holds mild value.*
- No sub props recommended — sub probability is negligible (<2%) for both fighters based on last-3 data.

**10. Fade Alert:**
- **Active:** Initial analysis leaned Ulberg as the superior fighter and value bet. After de-vig math, Ulberg at −300 (72.4% true prob) vs. assessed 62% probability = −10.4% edge — a **FADE ULBERG at current price.** Ulberg is a good fighter and likely wins, but at −300 the price is wrong for a bet. The value is on Prochazka at +250 as a speculative underdog play. The large sharp movement toward Ulberg is a contradicting signal — hence LEAN, not PLAY.

**FLIP CONDITIONS (Rule 15.1):**
- If Prochazka's chin damage from the two Pereira losses is more structurally severe than the stats suggest (e.g., confirmed neurological testing, camp sources noting sparring issues), switch to NO BET.
- If the line move from +250 to −300 on Ulberg was driven by confirmed injury news on Prochazka (not just sharp action), switch to NO BET.
- If Ulberg's in-camp striking has visibly improved (new striking coach, documented), maintain NO BET on Prochazka.
- If the line corrects back toward Prochazka +300 or longer before fight night, upgrade to PLAY.

Probability: 22%
Why: Both fighters are operating at suppressed output (last-3 SLpM under 2.5 each), making a slow, measured chess match the realistic base case if neither lands a defining shot early. Ulberg's superior strike defense (51% vs. 45%) and lower absorption rate (4.04 vs. 5.60 SApM) mean he wins the volume-and-accuracy war on the cards even without a finish. His three-fight decision run (Blachowicz, Oezdemir, now potentially Prochazka if the finish never materializes) fits this pattern.

**#3 — Jiri Prochazka wins by KO/TKO, Round 2–3**
Probability: 25%
Why: Prochazka's last two wins followed an identical pattern — he absorbed damage early, appeared in trouble, then produced a counter-striking finish late (Rountree R3 3:04, Hill R3 3:01). If Ulberg fails to close before R2, Prochazka's unorthodox angles and willingness to brawl at close range can generate the single clean shot he needs. The 3" reach advantage provides slightly more threat at distance, and Ulberg's own chin history (1 KO loss, Nzechukwu R2) suggests he is not immune to late-fight power.

**#4 — Carlos Ulberg wins by KO/TKO, Round 3**
Probability: 8%
Why: Lower probability but non-trivial: if Prochazka survives early rounds and the fight grinds into R3, Ulberg's superior cardio baseline (lower SApM = less damage accumulating) positions him to finish a worn-down Prochazka who may be running on fumes after trading early. Prochazka has never won a decision in his UFC career — if he hasn't finished by R3, he typically has nothing left but survival mode.

*(Probabilities: 35 + 22 + 25 + 8 = 90%; remaining 10% covers miscellaneous — submission by Prochazka, doctor stoppage, or split-decision outcomes.)*
<!--END-->

<!--BETTING-->
## Betting Recommendation

**1. TIER LABEL: PLAY**

**2. PRIMARY PICK: Carlos Ulberg — Confidence: 62%**

**3. Edge Breakdown:**
- Ulberg current line: −300
- Implied probability (−300): 300 / 400 = **75.0%**
- De-vig: Prochazka +250 → 100/350 = 28.6%. Sum = 75.0 + 28.6 = 103.6%. True prob Ulberg = 75.0 / 103.6 = **72.4%**. True prob Prochazka = 28.6 / 103.6 = **27.6%.**
- Assessed probability (Ulberg): **62%** (35% KO win R1–2 + 22% decision + 8% late KO, stacked from Section 8 math — total Ulberg outcomes = 65%, conservatively adjusted to 62% given MEDIUM data tier cap)
- **Edge: 62% − 72.4% = −10.4%**

⚠️ **EDGE IS NEGATIVE.** The market has Ulberg priced correctly — or more precisely, it has him overpriced. At −300, Ulberg's implied true probability (72.4%) is HIGHER than my assessed probability (62%). There is no value on Ulberg at this number.

**Re-checking Prochazka (+250):**
- True market prob for Prochazka: **27.6%**
- Assessed probability for Prochazka: outcomes 3+4 = 25% + some decision Prochazka scenarios. Total Prochazka win probability from the ENDINGS math: ~35% (25% KO + 10% miscellaneous/decision)
- Assessed prob Prochazka: **35%**
- Edge on Prochazka: 35% − 27.6% = **+7.4%**

**PRIMARY PICK CORRECTED (Rule 13.8 — Fade Alert):**
> *"Initial read: Ulberg. After de-vig and probability assessment: Prochazka at +250 has +7.4% edge. Switching recommendation to Prochazka."*

**REVISED PRIMARY PICK: Jiri Prochazka (+250) — Confidence: 60%**

**Revised Edge Breakdown:**
- Line: Prochazka +250
- Implied prob: 28.6%
- De-vigged true prob: 27.6%
- Assessed prob: 35%
- Edge: **+7.4%** ✅ (above the 5% PLAY threshold)

**4. Stake Calculation (Quarter Kelly):**
- Decimal odds for +250: (250/100) + 1 = 3.50
- Kelly fraction = (0.074 × 3.50 − (1 − 0.35)) / (3.50 − 1) = (0.259 − 0.65) / 2.50 = −0.391 / 2.50

⚠️ **Kelly fraction is NEGATIVE.** This means the bet is mathematically −EV at the assessed probability. Quarter Kelly does not override a negative Kelly result — when Kelly is negative, the bet has no correct stake size. The model assessed 35% win probability; for a +250 play to be Kelly-positive, you need assessed probability above (1 / 3.50) = **28.6%**, which is satisfied (35% > 28.6%). Let me recheck:

- Kelly = (b × p − q) / b where b = net odds decimal (2.50), p = 0.35, q = 0.65
- Kelly = (2.50 × 0.35 − 0.65) / 2.50 = (0.875 − 0.65) / 2.50 = 0.225 / 2.50 = **0.09 (9%)**
- Quarter Kelly = 0.09 × 0.25 = 0.0225 → **0.2 units** (rounded to one decimal)

*(Note: The full Kelly formula in Section 11.4 uses decimal_odds as the full return including stake, not net — recalculating correctly gives a small but positive Kelly fraction.)*

**Recommended stake: 0.2 units**

**5. Target Line:** For +3% edge minimum on Prochazka, market true prob must be < 32%. That requires Prochazka to be no worse than approximately **+215** (at which point de-vigged true prob ≈ ~30%). **Target: Prochazka at +215 or better. Current +250 clears this with margin.**

**6. Data Tiers:**
- Jiri Prochazka: **MEDIUM** (5+ UFC fights, full splits, recent activity, CHIN CRACKED flag active)
- Carlos Ulberg: **MEDIUM** (full UFC splits, recent activity, no critical missing stats)
- Confidence cap for MEDIUM/MEDIUM: **67% maximum** — current assessed confidence of 60% is within bounds.

**7. Market Read:**
- Prochazka: Open +250 → Current +250 — **FLAT. No movement.**
- Ulberg: Open +250 → Current −300 — **550-cent move toward Ulberg. Massive sharp action on Ulberg.** This is not public money — public doesn't move a line from +250 to −300 unilaterally. This is a sharp/syndicate move.
- RLM: NO — no reverse line movement flag. Line moved consistently toward Ulberg.
- The market read **disagrees with the Prochazka value pick.** Per Rule 10.11: "Stat-read disagrees with the market → −5% confidence penalty." Confidence adjusted: 60% → **55%** (per Rule 10.11 and Rule 13.5 — stat-read vs. confirmed large market move → cap at 55%).
- Per Rule 10.4: "If your stat-based read disagrees with the RLM, re-examine your read first." This is not technically RLM (line moved with, not against, the public direction), but the magnitude of the move (550 cents) functions as a sharp signal. **The edge identified may be illusory** — the market has dramatically re-priced Ulberg, and the movement magnitude suggests informed money with knowledge the public stats don't capture (camp quality, sparring reports, Prochazka's physical condition post-two-KO-losses).

**REVISED TIER: LEAN (not PLAY)** — Confidence drops to 55% after applying the −5% market-disagreement penalty. Per Rule 13.2: 55–60% = LEAN. Per Rule 13.7: a PLAY requires ≥60% assessed prob AND ≥5% edge. The confidence is now at 55%, falling below the PLAY threshold.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION: LEAN — Jiri Prochazka (+250). NOT a full play. Small speculative position only at current line.**
- Confidence: **55%**
- Edge on paper: +7.4% (but confidence cap fires due to large market movement against the pick)
- Stake: **0 units formally (LEAN, not PLAY). If taking the speculative position: maximum 0.5 units.** Do not exceed.
- Target: +215 or better

**8. Hedge Verdict:**
- Best available: Ulberg −300 (FanDuel/DraftKings approximate), Prochazka +250
- Combined implied: 75.0% + 28.6% = **103.6%** → **NO ARB.** Book margin is 3.6%. No hedging structure produces profit. Take the value pick only.
- **VERDICT: NO ARB.**

**9. Props Worth Considering:**
- **"Fight goes to decision" — YES:** Section 8.4 calculated decision probability at ~30%. If this prop is available at implied probability below 30% (e.g., YES at +200 = 33.3% implied, or de-vigged below 30%), there is marginal value. Both fighters' last-3 SLpM data (Prochazka avg 1.57, Ulberg avg 1.69) confirm suppressed output — in a low-pace brawl, finishing becomes less likely. *Check props lines at your book; value exists only if the YES line is +200 or longer.*
- **"Fight ends in Round 1 — NO":** Both fighters' most recent finishes came in R1 (Ulberg over Reyes, 4:27) and R3 (Prochazka over Rountree, 3:04). Prochazka has never been finished in R1 in UFC. R1 KO probability is lower than the market typically assigns. *If this NO is available at reasonable vig, it holds mild value.*
- No sub props recommended — sub probability is negligible (<2%) for both fighters based on last-3 data.

**10. Fade Alert:**
- **Active:** Initial analysis leaned Ulberg as the superior fighter and value bet. After de-vig math, Ulberg at −300 (72.4% true prob) vs. assessed 62% probability = −10.4% edge — a **FADE ULBERG at current price.** Ulberg is a good fighter and likely wins, but at −300 the price is wrong for a bet. The value is on Prochazka at +250 as a speculative underdog play. The large sharp movement toward Ulberg is a contradicting signal — hence LEAN, not PLAY.

**FLIP CONDITIONS (Rule 15.1):**
- If Prochazka's chin damage from the two Pereira losses is more structurally severe than the stats suggest (e.g., confirmed neurological testing, camp sources noting sparring issues), switch to NO BET.
- If the line move from +250 to −300 on Ulberg was driven by confirmed injury news on Prochazka (not just sharp action), switch to NO BET.
- If Ulberg's in-camp striking has visibly improved (new striking coach, documented), maintain NO BET on Prochazka.
- If the line corrects back toward Prochazka +300 or longer before fight night, upgrade to PLAY.
<!--END-->

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Azamat Murzakanov -- Style & Profile

- **Data Tier: HIGH.** 7 UFC fights, full career splits available, active (164-day layoff). Archetype: **Power Striker** — 6 KO/TKO wins in last 7, SLpM 4.70 career, 57% striking accuracy, TDs negligible (0.55/15min, 15% accuracy). Direction: **FORWARD** — pressure-based KO threat who walks opponents down.
- **Elite KO efficiency with extreme variability:** Last-3 SLpM splits are wildly inconsistent — 0.94 (Rakic), 7.9 (Ribeiro), 2.65 (Menifield). This isn't a volume striker; he hunts one clean shot. Rakic fight (0.94 SLpM, R1 KO at 3:11) confirms he can win with minimal output if he lands the right punch.
- **Southpaw with elite strike defense (60%) and best-in-class TDD (87%):** The defensive profile is exceptional for a power striker — he doesn't just swing, he doesn't get hit and he neutralizes wrestling threats. Career SApM of 2.86 is excellent at LHW for someone who fights at close range.
- **Age cliff alert — 36 years old at LHW:** Per Rule 9.7, Middleweight–LHW cliff is 36. Murzakanov is exactly at the cliff. Apply −5% intangible penalty. His all-KO finish record also means we have zero data on how he performs past R2 — 6 of 7 wins are inside distance and all recent wins are R1 or R2. No evidence of late-round durability.
- **16-0 with zero losses — chin unproven at elite level:** IRON CHIN cannot be formally flagged (Rule 6.5 requires surviving known KO artists cleanly). His opponents — Rakic, Menifield, Jacoby — are solid but not historically elite one-shot KO artists. Chin is unproven against genuine power. This is a material gap in his profile going into a fight with Costa.
- **Layoff: 164 days** — within the 90–180 day "standard turnaround" window. No layoff penalty fires.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Paulo Costa -- Style & Profile

- **Data Tier: HIGH.** 10 UFC fights on record, full career splits available, 262-day layoff (270–365 day tier: −6% intangible penalty applies). Archetype: **Volume Striker in severe decline** — career SLpM 6.26 but last-3 SLpM average is just 1.77 (Kopylov 2.27, Strickland 1.24, Whittaker 1.80). Direction: **FORWARD** historically, but recent fight history shows a fighter who now primarily operates defensively.
- **CRITICAL TREND ALERT — Volume collapse:** Career SLpM 6.26 vs. last-3 average 1.77 — **TRENDING DOWN −72%.** This is not a rounding error; this is a fundamentally different fighter. He is no longer a high-volume pressure striker. He is a defensive decision-fighter who occasionally finds openings. The market may still be pricing the "Borrachinha" identity from 2019–2021.
- **Defensive decay confirmed (Rule 3.3):** Career SApM 6.20 — the highest of any fighter in this matchup by a wide margin. Career strike defense 49% — below the 50% threshold and **TRENDING DOWN** contextually (he has been outworked in 4 of his last 6 fights). **CHIN-DECAY ALERT partially fired:** 1 KO loss (Adesanya R2, 2021), plus cumulative damage from wars with Romero, Vettori, Strickland — all high-volume fights. At 34 with heavy accumulated damage, CHIN CRACKED cannot be formally confirmed (only 1 KO loss, 4 years ago) but the risk profile is elevated.
- **Win-method collapse — no finishes in last 6 fights:** Last 10 fights: 3 KO/TKO, 0 subs, 3 decisions — but the 3 KOs are all pre-2022. In his last 6 fights he has gone 3-3 with zero finishes. The "puncher's threat" narrative is outdated; his adjusted KO probability against a defensively sound opponent is materially lower than his career rate suggests.
- **Layoff: 262 days** — triggers the 270–365-day tier borderline. At 262 days it falls just inside the 180–270 "mild ring rust" range. Apply **−3% layoff penalty.** However, combined with the other intangibles, total penalty is tracked below.
- **Intangible stack:** Age 34 (women's cliff at 33 — not applicable; men's MW–LHW cliff at 36 — not yet fired). Layoff −3%. Weight class context: Costa fought at MW his entire UFC career and moved to LHW; this is his second LHW fight (Kopylov was his first). The weight class adjustment data is thin. Total intangible penalty: **−3%** (layoff only; age cliff not yet triggered at 34).
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

This is a **FORWARD vs. FORWARD** matchup — two pressure fighters who walk opponents down. Per Rule 4.4, when two FORWARDs meet, expect early exchanges and lean toward stoppage outcomes rather than decisions. The critical structural question is: whose version of a pressure striker is actually showing up in 2025? Murzakanov is a legitimate, currently operating pressure striker with 3 consecutive R1/R2 KOs. Costa is a *former* pressure striker whose last-3 SLpM (1.77 average) suggests he has fundamentally changed — or deteriorated — into a counterpunching decision-fighter. That distinction is enormous.

**Style matrix call (Rule 5.1 — Power Striker vs. Power Striker):** Per the matrix, the outcome collapses to "whoever lands first." Murzakanov's strike defense (60%) significantly outperforms Costa's (49%). Murzakanov's SApM (2.86) is dramatically better than Costa's career number (6.20). The structural edge belongs to Murzakanov — he lands more cleanly (57% accuracy vs. 58% career for Costa, but Costa's last-3 accuracy at 63% with minimal volume is a false positive), absorbs far less, and finishes at a higher rate against comparable opposition. Costa's TDD (80%) is adequate but Murzakanov's grappling is irrelevant (0.55 TD/15min, 15% accuracy) — this fight stays standing. The TDD master variable (Rule 5.2) does not apply here; neither fighter wrestles meaningfully.

**The southpaw edge (Rule 5.4):** Murzakanov is southpaw vs. Costa's orthodox. Critical check: of Costa's last 5 fights — Kopylov (orthodox), Strickland (orthodox), Whittaker (orthodox), Rockhold (orthodox), Vettori (orthodox) — **zero southpaw opponents in his last 5 fights.** The southpaw edge is **real and unmitigated.** Murzakanov's left hand from the southpaw stance presents angles Costa has not navigated recently. This is a secondary structural edge that compounds the volume/defense gap. Net intangible delta: Murzakanov −5% (age cliff penalty) vs. Costa −3% (layoff). **Net delta favors Murzakanov by +2 percentage points** from intangibles — a negligible gap that does not change the structural read.

**Flip conditions (Rule 15.1):** Switch to Costa if: (1) post-weigh-in reports show Murzakanov drained from a difficult cut suggesting weight-cut fatigue; (2) tape emerges showing Murzakanov's recent KO speed is due to opponent errors rather than genuine power development; (3) Costa's camp change or preparation specifically addressed southpaw work. Switch to NO BET if Murzakanov's age-related decline accelerates and is reflected in a dramatic line lengthening past −150.
analysis_sections.endings:
## 3-5 Most Likely Fight Endings (Ranked by Probability)

**#1 — Murzakanov wins by KO/TKO, Round 1 or 2**
Probability: 42%
Why: Murzakanov has finished 3 consecutive opponents in R1/R2, and Costa's career SApM of 6.20 means he absorbs punishment at an extraordinarily high rate. Adjusted KO probability: base KO rate = 6 KOs in 7 fights = 0.857 × (1 − 0.49 str. def.) × 1.0 (chin modifier — CHIN CRACKED not formally confirmed) × 1.0 (3-round format) ≈ 0.857 × 0.51 ≈ **43.7%.** Southpaw angles further elevate the R1/R2 probability given Costa's zero recent southpaw experience.

**#2 — Costa wins by U-DEC, Round 3**
Probability: 24%
Why: Costa's last-3 pattern is survive-and-decide — he went the distance against Kopylov, Strickland, and Whittaker, demonstrating durability despite taking damage. If Murzakanov's volume stays low (as it did vs. Rakic at 0.94 SLpM), Costa's higher accuracy in recent fights (63–72%) allows him to outwork Murzakanov on the cards across three rounds. This path requires Murzakanov to miss frequently and Costa to stay disciplined rather than engaging in a firefight.

**#3 — Murzakanov wins by U-DEC, Round 3**
Probability: 18%
Why: Murzakanov went to a decision against Dustin Jacoby and clearly has the capacity to grind out a fight when finishing opportunities don't materialize. Against a Costa who is operating at 1.77 SLpM (last-3), low output from both men could produce a decision victory for Murzakanov via cage control and cleaner striking. Decision probability from Section 8.4: 1 − 0.437 (Murzakanov KO) − 0.04 (Costa KO, heavily discounted given no finishes in 6 fights and Murzakanov's 60% str. def.) − 0.0 (sub both ways) ≈ 52% decision probability total; split roughly 18% Murzakanov decision / 24% Costa decision based on structural edge.

**#4 — Costa wins by KO/TKO, Round 1 or 2**
Probability: 8%
Why: Costa has not finished anyone since 2022, and Murzakanov's 60% strike defense significantly depresses the KO probability. Adjusted Costa KO prob: base rate last-6 = 0/6 (zero finishes) — using career rate adjusted: ~30% career KO rate × (1 − 0.60 Murzakanov str. def.) × 1.0 (chin unproven, not IRON CHIN formally) = 0.30 × 0.40 = **12%,** discounted further for Costa's volume collapse to approximately **8%.** Not zero — Murzakanov's chin is genuinely unproven against real power — but heavily discounted.
analysis_sections.betting:
## Betting Recommendation

**TIER: PLAY**
**Primary Pick: Azamat Murzakanov — Confidence: 62%**

---

**Edge Breakdown:**

- Current line: Murzakanov −219 (current) / Costa +190 (current)
- Murzakanov implied prob (−219): 219 / (219 + 100) = **68.7%**
- Costa implied prob (+190): 100 / (190 + 100) = **34.5%**
- Sum: 68.7 + 34.5 = **103.2%** (3.2% vig)
- De-vigged true prob Murzakanov: 68.7 / 103.2 = **66.6%**
- De-vigged true prob Costa: 34.5 / 103.2 = **33.4%**
- **Assessed probability Murzakanov: 62%**
- **Edge: 62% − 66.6% = −4.6%** ← Murzakanov moneyline has **NEGATIVE edge** at current price.

**The moneyline on Murzakanov at −219 has no value. The market is overpricing him by ~4.6 percentage points.**

---

**Switching sides — Fade alert (Rule 13.8):**

Costa at +190 implies 34.5% (de-vigged: 33.4%). Assessed Costa probability: **38%.**
- **Edge on Costa: 38% − 33.4% = +4.6%**
- This clears the 3% noise threshold and qualifies as a marginal play.

*"Initial read: Murzakanov structural favorite. After de-vig and line math: Costa +190 has +4.6% edge. The market is short enough on Murzakanov that the line overstates his true probability. Switching recommendation to Costa +190 as the value side."*

---

**Quarter-Kelly stake calculation (Costa +190):**
- Decimal odds: (190/100) + 1 = **2.90**
- kelly_fraction = (0.046 × 2.90 − (1 − 0.38)) / (2.90 − 1)
- = (0.1334 − 0.62) / 1.90
- = (−0.4866) / 1.90 = **−0.256**

Quarter Kelly is **negative.** Per Rule 11.4: *"If quarter Kelly comes out negative, the bet is mathematically -EV — do not place it."*

---

**Revised recommendation: NO BET**

The math is unambiguous. Costa at +190 has positive edge (+4.6%) on a straight probability assessment, but the Kelly formula returns negative because the odds-adjusted expected value still doesn't overcome the win probability gap. The market has this fight priced efficiently enough that neither side clears a Kelly-positive bet.

**Tier: NO BET**
**Pick: PASS**
**Edge: Murzakanov −4.6% (overpriced), Costa +4.6% (inside Kelly viability threshold)**
**Stake: 0 units**
**Target line for Murzakanov: Would need −155 or shorter to have +3% edge**
**Target line for Costa: Would need +220 or longer to have +5% edge (Kelly-positive)**

---

**Data Tiers:**
- Murzakanov: **HIGH** (7 UFC fights, active, full splits)
- Costa: **HIGH** (10 UFC fights, active, full splits)
- No confidence cap fires from data tier. Edge calculation, not data quality, drives the NO BET.

**Active confidence caps:** None from data tier. Rule 13.7 fires: "High confidence, low Kelly-positive edge = NO BET." Having an assessed read of 62% Murzakanov does not create a playable bet when the line is −219.

---

**Market Read:**
- Open: Murzakanov −230 / Costa +200. Current: Murzakanov −219 / Costa +190.
- Movement: Both lines have **lengthened** — Murzakanov moved from −230 to −219 (lengthened, meaning money came in on Costa), Costa moved from +200 to +190 (also lengthened — this is contradictory line behavior that suggests cross-book variance or data artifact).
- RLM: **NO** (confirmed per data provided).
- Interpretation: Line has softened slightly on Murzakanov (opened shorter, now longer) — mild money on Costa early in the week. Not a strong signal either direction.
- Market read **partially agrees** with my structural assessment (Murzakanov is the right favorite) but the current price is too short for value.

---

**Hedge Verdict:**
- Best line F1 (Murzakanov): −219 → implied 68.7%
- Best line F2 (Costa): +190 → implied 34.5%
- Combined implied: **103.2%**
- **NO ARB** — combined implied exceeds 100%. Do not hedge. Take the value pick on the best available line — but as established above, neither side clears the Kelly-positive threshold at current prices.

---

**Props worth considering:**

*Fight goes to decision:* From Section 8.4 calculation, decision probability ≈ **42%** (18% Murzakanov decision + 24% Costa decision). If the market is pricing "fight goes to distance" at longer than +138 (implied 42%), there is value on the YES side. Costa's last 3 fights all went to decision. Murzakanov's only career decision was Jacoby. At +130 or longer for "fight goes to distance" — **marginal value YES.** De-vig required before betting: treat as a 5%+ edge threshold for props per Rule 11.7. Monitor the prop line; if available at +140+ (implied 41.7%), edge clears 0.3% above assessed 42% — still thin. **No prop value confirmed without verifying the decision prop line.**

**Final recommendation: NO BET on moneyline. Monitor decision prop — value possible at +140 or longer.**

The critical structural question is: whose version of a pressure striker is actually showing up in 2025? Murzakanov is a legitimate, currently operating pressure striker with 3 consecutive R1/R2 KOs. Costa is a *former* pressure striker whose last-3 SLpM (1.77 average) suggests he has fundamentally changed — or deteriorated — into a counterpunching decision-fighter. That distinction is enormous.

**Style matrix call (Rule 5.1 — Power Striker vs. Power Striker):** Per the matrix, the outcome collapses to "whoever lands first." Murzakanov's strike defense (60%) significantly outperforms Costa's (49%). Murzakanov's SApM (2.86) is dramatically better than Costa's career number (6.20). The structural edge belongs to Murzakanov — he lands more cleanly (57% accuracy vs. 58% career for Costa, but Costa's last-3 accuracy at 63% with minimal volume is a false positive), absorbs far less, and finishes at a higher rate against comparable opposition. Costa's TDD (80%) is adequate but Murzakanov's grappling is irrelevant (0.55 TD/15min, 15% accuracy) — this fight stays standing. The TDD master variable (Rule 5.2) does not apply here; neither fighter wrestles meaningfully.

**The southpaw edge (Rule 5.4):** Murzakanov is southpaw vs. Costa's orthodox. Critical check: of Costa's last 5 fights — Kopylov (orthodox), Strickland (orthodox), Whittaker (orthodox), Rockhold (orthodox), Vettori (orthodox) — **zero southpaw opponents in his last 5 fights.** The southpaw edge is **real and unmitigated.** Murzakanov's left hand from the southpaw stance presents angles Costa has not navigated recently. This is a secondary structural edge that compounds the volume/defense gap. Net intangible delta: Murzakanov −5% (age cliff penalty) vs. Costa −3% (layoff). **Net delta favors Murzakanov by +2 percentage points** from intangibles — a negligible gap that does not change the structural read.

**Flip conditions (Rule 15.1):** Switch to Costa if: (1) post-weigh-in reports show Murzakanov drained from a difficult cut suggesting weight-cut fatigue; (2) tape emerges showing Murzakanov's recent KO speed is due to opponent errors rather than genuine power development; (3) Costa's camp change or preparation specifically addressed southpaw work. Switch to NO BET if Murzakanov's age-related decline accelerates and is reflected in a dramatic line lengthening past −150.

<!--END-->

<!--ENDINGS-->
## 3-5 Most Likely Fight Endings (Ranked by Probability)

**#1 — Murzakanov wins by KO/TKO, Round 1 or 2**
Probability: 42%
Why: Murzakanov has finished 3 consecutive opponents in R1/R2, and Costa's career SApM of 6.20 means he absorbs punishment at an extraordinarily high rate. Adjusted KO probability: base KO rate = 6 KOs in 7 fights = 0.857 × (1 − 0.49 str. def.) × 1.0 (chin modifier — CHIN CRACKED not formally confirmed) × 1.0 (3-round format) ≈ 0.857 × 0.51 ≈ **43.7%.** Southpaw angles further elevate the R1/R2 probability given Costa's zero recent southpaw experience.

**#2 — Costa wins by U-DEC, Round 3**
Probability: 24%
Why: Costa's last-3 pattern is survive-and-decide — he went the distance against Kopylov, Strickland, and Whittaker, demonstrating durability despite taking damage. If Murzakanov's volume stays low (as it did vs. Rakic at 0.94 SLpM), Costa's higher accuracy in recent fights (63–72%) allows him to outwork Murzakanov on the cards across three rounds. This path requires Murzakanov to miss frequently and Costa to stay disciplined rather than engaging in a firefight.

**#3 — Murzakanov wins by U-DEC, Round 3**
Probability: 18%
Why: Murzakanov went to a decision against Dustin Jacoby and clearly has the capacity to grind out a fight when finishing opportunities don't materialize. Against a Costa who is operating at 1.77 SLpM (last-3), low output from both men could produce a decision victory for Murzakanov via cage control and cleaner striking. Decision probability from Section 8.4: 1 − 0.437 (Murzakanov KO) − 0.04 (Costa KO, heavily discounted given no finishes in 6 fights and Murzakanov's 60% str. def.) − 0.0 (sub both ways) ≈ 52% decision probability total; split roughly 18% Murzakanov decision / 24% Costa decision based on structural edge.

**#4 — Costa wins by KO/TKO, Round 1 or 2**
Probability: 8%
Why: Costa has not finished anyone since 2022, and Murzakanov's 60% strike defense significantly depresses the KO probability. Adjusted Costa KO prob: base rate last-6 = 0/6 (zero finishes) — using career rate adjusted: ~30% career KO rate × (1 − 0.60 Murzakanov str. def.) × 1.0 (chin unproven, not IRON CHIN formally) = 0.30 × 0.40 = **12%,** discounted further for Costa's volume collapse to approximately **8%.** Not zero — Murzakanov's chin is genuinely unproven against real power — but heavily discounted.

<!--END-->

<!--BETTING-->
## Betting Recommendation

**TIER: PLAY**
**Primary Pick: Azamat Murzakanov — Confidence: 62%**

---

**Edge Breakdown:**

- Current line: Murzakanov −219 (current) / Costa +190 (current)
- Murzakanov implied prob (−219): 219 / (219 + 100) = **68.7%**
- Costa implied prob (+190): 100 / (190 + 100) = **34.5%**
- Sum: 68.7 + 34.5 = **103.2%** (3.2% vig)
- De-vigged true prob Murzakanov: 68.7 / 103.2 = **66.6%**
- De-vigged true prob Costa: 34.5 / 103.2 = **33.4%**
- **Assessed probability Murzakanov: 62%**
- **Edge: 62% − 66.6% = −4.6%** ← Murzakanov moneyline has **NEGATIVE edge** at current price.

**The moneyline on Murzakanov at −219 has no value. The market is overpricing him by ~4.6 percentage points.**

---

**Switching sides — Fade alert (Rule 13.8):**

Costa at +190 implies 34.5% (de-vigged: 33.4%). Assessed Costa probability: **38%.**
- **Edge on Costa: 38% − 33.4% = +4.6%**
- This clears the 3% noise threshold and qualifies as a marginal play.

*"Initial read: Murzakanov structural favorite. After de-vig and line math: Costa +190 has +4.6% edge. The market is short enough on Murzakanov that the line overstates his true probability. Switching recommendation to Costa +190 as the value side."*

---

**Quarter-Kelly stake calculation (Costa +190):**
- Decimal odds: (190/100) + 1 = **2.90**
- kelly_fraction = (0.046 × 2.90 − (1 − 0.38)) / (2.90 − 1)
- = (0.1334 − 0.62) / 1.90
- = (−0.4866) / 1.90 = **−0.256**

Quarter Kelly is **negative.** Per Rule 11.4: *"If quarter Kelly comes out negative, the bet is mathematically -EV — do not place it."*

---

**Revised recommendation: NO BET**

The math is unambiguous. Costa at +190 has positive edge (+4.6%) on a straight probability assessment, but the Kelly formula returns negative because the odds-adjusted expected value still doesn't overcome the win probability gap. The market has this fight priced efficiently enough that neither side clears a Kelly-positive bet.

**Tier: NO BET**
**Pick: PASS**
**Edge: Murzakanov −4.6% (overpriced), Costa +4.6% (inside Kelly viability threshold)**
**Stake: 0 units**
**Target line for Murzakanov: Would need −155 or shorter to have +3% edge**
**Target line for Costa: Would need +220 or longer to have +5% edge (Kelly-positive)**

---

**Data Tiers:**
- Murzakanov: **HIGH** (7 UFC fights, active, full splits)
- Costa: **HIGH** (10 UFC fights, active, full splits)
- No confidence cap fires from data tier. Edge calculation, not data quality, drives the NO BET.

**Active confidence caps:** None from data tier. Rule 13.7 fires: "High confidence, low Kelly-positive edge = NO BET." Having an assessed read of 62% Murzakanov does not create a playable bet when the line is −219.

---

**Market Read:**
- Open: Murzakanov −230 / Costa +200. Current: Murzakanov −219 / Costa +190.
- Movement: Both lines have **lengthened** — Murzakanov moved from −230 to −219 (lengthened, meaning money came in on Costa), Costa moved from +200 to +190 (also lengthened — this is contradictory line behavior that suggests cross-book variance or data artifact).
- RLM: **NO** (confirmed per data provided).
- Interpretation: Line has softened slightly on Murzakanov (opened shorter, now longer) — mild money on Costa early in the week. Not a strong signal either direction.
- Market read **partially agrees** with my structural assessment (Murzakanov is the right favorite) but the current price is too short for value.

---

**Hedge Verdict:**
- Best line F1 (Murzakanov): −219 → implied 68.7%
- Best line F2 (Costa): +190 → implied 34.5%
- Combined implied: **103.2%**
- **NO ARB** — combined implied exceeds 100%. Do not hedge. Take the value pick on the best available line — but as established above, neither side clears the Kelly-positive threshold at current prices.

---

**Props worth considering:**

*Fight goes to decision:* From Section 8.4 calculation, decision probability ≈ **42%** (18% Murzakanov decision + 24% Costa decision). If the market is pricing "fight goes to distance" at longer than +138 (implied 42%), there is value on the YES side. Costa's last 3 fights all went to decision. Murzakanov's only career decision was Jacoby. At +130 or longer for "fight goes to distance" — **marginal value YES.** De-vig required before betting: treat as a 5%+ edge threshold for props per Rule 11.7. Monitor the prop line; if available at +140+ (implied 41.7%), edge clears 0.3% above assessed 42% — still thin. **No prop value confirmed without verifying the decision prop line.**

**Final recommendation: NO BET on moneyline. Monitor decision prop — value possible at +140 or longer.**

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Curtis Blaydes -- Style & Profile

- **Data Tier: MEDIUM** — 5+ UFC fights but significant stat volatility across last-3 splits (SLpM ranges from 0.0 to 5.0 across three fights), layoff of 290 days, and missing Tapology intangibles data. Career averages treated as suspect for trend purposes.
- **Archetype: Pressure Wrestler / Clinch Power Striker (FORWARD)** — Career 5.38 TD/15min is elite-level wrestling volume at heavyweight. However, last-3 shows near-zero grappling output (TD/15: 2.0, 0.0, 0.0), suggesting he is increasingly fighting as a striker. Win method breakdown (4 KO/TKO, 0 subs, 3 decisions in last 10) confirms a shift toward striking-based finishes.
- **CHIN CRACKED ALERT** — 3 KO/TKO losses in last 5 fights (Aspinall R1, Pavlovich R1, Derrick Lewis R2). Two of the three came in Round 1, and one (Pavlovich) came from a non-elite finisher by historical standards. Criteria from Rule 6.2 clearly met: 2+ KO losses in last 3 years, most recent KO in R1, and he is 35 years old (heavyweight age cliff triggers at 37 but chin decay at 35+ is real). **Flag: CHIN CRACKED.**
- **Age cliff at 35 (HW)** — Rule 9.7 places the heavyweight cliff at 37, meaning the formal penalty does not yet fire. However, the chin-crack pattern at 35 is behaviorally equivalent. The Aspinall rematch (R1 KO) and Pavlovich loss (R1 KO) are concerning patterns regardless of the formal cliff threshold.
- **Stat-trend analysis — CAUTION FLAGS:** SLpM last-3 average: (0.8 + 5.0 + 0.0) / 3 = **1.93 vs. career 3.56 — TRENDING DOWN (−46%)**. TD/15 last-3: (2.0 + 0.0 + 0.0) / 3 = **0.67 vs. career 5.38 — TRENDING DOWN (−88%)**. The grappling output has essentially vanished. Str. Acc. last-3 (excluding the 0% Almeida fight which was a 36-second blitz): roughly 49% — near flat. SApM data too variable to trend cleanly, but absorbing a R1 KO from Aspinall is a significant data point.
- **Layoff: 290 days** — Falls in the 270–365-day bucket (Rule 9.2): **−6% win probability penalty**. Fight against Kuniev (June 2025) was a split decision win, suggesting he was not at peak form coming off prior inactivity.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Josh Hokit -- Style & Profile

- **Data Tier: LOW** — Only 3 UFC fights, all wins, no losses to reference for durability. Pre-UFC record is 5-0 but opponent quality is unverifiable as regional/Contender Series level. All analysis carries LOW-tier confidence cap: **maximum 60% assessed probability (LEAN only, no PLAY).**
- **Archetype: Pressure Wrestler / Volume Striker (FORWARD)** — Career SLpM of 6.07 with 66% striking accuracy is elite output. TD/15 of 8.74 is extremely high. However, last-3 splits show wide variance: SLpM ranged from 0.66 to 9.64, suggesting his style adapts heavily to opponent resistance. He appears to prefer striking but will shoot heavily when striking is working (Uriel fight: 12.3 TD/15, Gimenis fight: 0 TDs but 9.64 SLpM KO).
- **100% TD defense in UFC** — Three fights, zero successful takedowns allowed. This is a meaningful early signal but the sample (3 fights) is too small to declare it career-defining. Treat as notable but not fully reliable.
- **Finishing ability is striking-based, not submission-based** — 3 KO/TKO wins, 0 submissions in UFC career. Sub Avg. is 0.0. Despite elite grappling volume, he uses takedowns as setup for ground-and-pound, not for submission hunting. Secondary tendency: clinch wrestling to top control, finish by strikes.
- **Opponent quality — DISCOUNT REQUIRED** — Guilherme Uriel, Max Gimenis, Denzel Freeman are all unranked UFC fighters with limited records. Per Rule 3.2, Hokit's impressive stats (6.07 SLpM, 66% Str. Acc., 8.74 TD/15) must be heavily discounted until tested against meaningful competition. Flag: **"Trending up against unranked opponents — discount heavily."**
- **Fresh, sharp** — 73-day layoff, no weight issues indicated, 28 years old. No intangibles penalties apply. No chin data available (never been in serious trouble in 3 UFC fights).
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

**Matrix outcome: Pressure Wrestler (Hokit) vs. Pressure Wrestler / Clinch Striker (Blaydes).** Two FORWARD fighters meeting — Rule 4.4 signals to expect an aggressive early pace. The matrix for mirror-archetype pressure wrestlers collapses to the TD defense variable (Rule 5.2). Blaydes' TD defense is listed at 31% career — dramatically below the 60% threshold at which the stat-based edge flips toward the wrestler. Hokit's 8.74 TD/15min career average combined with Blaydes' 31% TD defense creates a structural opening for Hokit to take this fight to the ground at will. However, Hokit's grappling is primarily ground-and-pound oriented, and Blaydes — when healthy and not chinned — is an elite wrestler himself who knows how to recover position. The key variable is whether Blaydes can use his 7-inch reach advantage to keep Hokit at the end of his punches and deny the takedown entries.

**The chin is the decisive variable.** Blaydes has been CHIN CRACKED by two of the better fighters in the division (Aspinall, Pavlovich) plus Derrick Lewis before them — all via strikes. Hokit is a 3-fight KO artist averaging 6.07 SLpM with 66% accuracy. Against a fighter who absorbs KOs in Round 1, this is exactly the structural mismatch that Rule 6.2 was designed to flag. Blaydes' 7-inch reach advantage matters in the abstract, but his increasingly dormant grappling (TD/15 effectively zero in last-3) means he is not using his primary defensive tool (level changes, forward wrestling pressure) to disrupt strikers. The Aspinall R1 KO and Pavlovich R1 KO both followed Blaydes trying to strike from the outside and absorbing clean counters. Hokit's forward pressure style would not give Blaydes the same long-range countering opportunity he typically prefers.

**Path to victory for Blaydes:** Use the 7-inch reach advantage to keep Hokit at range, land his power right hand (which has been his primary KO weapon historically), and attempt to revive the wrestling game that has been absent in last-3. If he can stuff Hokit's early takedown attempts, the experience gap and quality-of-opponent gap favor a Blaydes decision or late KO. The critical flip point: Blaydes needs to NOT absorb a clean early shot, because the chin data says he does not recover from those anymore. **Path to victory for Hokit:** Press forward immediately, close the reach gap with level changes (Hokit's 31% TD defense on Blaydes means he gets doubles and singles whenever he commits), mix strikes with wrestling to prevent Blaydes from setting his feet, and look for the finish in the first or second round before any experience/craft advantage can manifest. **Net intangible delta: Hokit has a −6% layoff advantage** (Hokit: 0 intangible penalties; Blaydes: −6% for 290-day layoff, plus CHIN CRACKED flag). The data clearly leans toward Hokit as the structural and physical threat — but the LOW data tier on Hokit forces hard confidence caps.
analysis_sections.endings:
## 3-5 Most Likely Fight Endings (Ranked by Probability)

**Section 8 math inputs:**
- Hokit base KO rate: 3 KO wins / 8 total fights = 37.5% raw. However, vs. UFC opponents only: 3/3 = 100%, but sample is 3 fights — discount toward ~40% per Rule 8.5 opponent quality concern.
- Blaydes Str. Def.: 58% (career). Chin modifier: 1.4 (CHIN CRACKED). Cardio modifier: 1.0 (unknown rounds, assume 3).
- **Adjusted Hokit KO prob = 0.40 × (1 − 0.58) × 1.4 × 1.0 = 0.40 × 0.42 × 1.4 = 0.235 ≈ 24%**

- Blaydes base KO rate: 4 KO wins / 19 total fights = 21%. Vs. ranked: lower — approximately 15% defensible.
- Hokit Str. Def.: 66% (career, small sample). Chin modifier: 1.0 (no data on chin durability). Cardio modifier: 1.0.
- **Adjusted Blaydes KO prob on Hokit = 0.15 × (1 − 0.66) × 1.0 = 0.15 × 0.34 = 0.051 ≈ 5%**

- Blaydes base TD ability: 5.38 TD/15 career, but last-3 average: 0.67 TD/15 — trend near zero. Hokit TD defense 100% in 3 UFC fights. Sub rate: 0.0. Adjusted submission threat from Blaydes: negligible (< 2%).

- Decision probability = 1 − 0.24 (Hokit KO) − 0.05 (Blaydes KO) − 0.02 (other finishes) = **~69% decision/non-KO**. This seems high given both fighters' finishing tendencies, but reflects Blaydes' durability in decision fights (has gone 3 and 5 rounds multiple times) and the uncertainty of a low-data Hokit. Splitting this into decision for Hokit and decision for Blaydes based on overall stylistic read.

---

**#1 — Josh Hokit wins by KO/TKO, Round 1 or 2**
Probability: 30%
Why: The CHIN CRACKED flag on Blaydes combined with Hokit's 6.07 SLpM and 66% accuracy creates the highest-probability path to a finish. Blaydes has absorbed R1 KOs from Aspinall and Pavlovich — both times when absorbing a sharp counter — and Hokit's forward pressure style will close the reach gap quickly. The 7-inch reach disadvantage does not help a fighter with Blaydes' established chin fragility if he cannot keep Hokit at range for the full fight. Hokit has finished all 3 UFC opponents, two in Round 1.

**#2 — Curtis Blaydes wins by Decision**
Probability: 28%
Why: Blaydes' experience gap (19 fights vs. 8), reach advantage (7 inches), and historical ability to outwork opponents over 15 minutes (3 decision wins in last 10, including a 5-rounder vs. Volkov) give him a legitimate path to outpointing a fighter who has never been tested past Round 2. If Blaydes uses the jab to maintain range, stuffs the early takedown attempts, and avoids the first clean exchange, his craft and durability in non-finish scenarios could get him through 3 rounds. Hokit's opponent quality does not include anyone who can test game-plan execution over a full fight.

**#3 — Josh Hokit wins by Decision**
Probability: 22%
Why: If Hokit cannot finish Blaydes early, his forward pressure and 8.74 TD/15 career average means he can control the fight on the ground through all three rounds. Blaydes' 31% TD defense and near-zero recent grappling output (TD/15 of 0.67 in last-3) mean Hokit can take him down repeatedly. Even without a finish, consistent top control against a shot wrestler at 35 with chin concerns likely wins rounds on the judges' cards.

**#4 — Curtis Blaydes wins by KO/TKO**
Probability: 7%
Why: Blaydes has legitimate power (4 KOs in last 10, including a 15-second R1 KO of Tom Aspinall in their first fight). If the reach advantage allows him to land the right hand before Hokit closes the distance, an early finish is possible. However, the adjusted KO probability of ~5% against an untested chin and Hokit's 100% TD defense in UFC makes this a low-probability path. The experience gap and power history are the only things keeping this scenario on the board.

- Implied probability: 100 / (140 + 100) = **41.7%**
- De-vig: Blaydes −138 → implied = 138 / (138 + 100) = 57.98%; Hokit +140 → 41.7%; Sum = 99.68%; De-vig true Hokit = 41.7 / 99.68 = **41.84%**

Re-examining: The edge is inflated by Hokit's LOW data tier, meaning the 58% assessed probability itself is carrying significant uncertainty. The LOW data cap is already applied (preventing a higher confidence call), but the raw edge number reflects a genuinely mispriced underdog line. Still, a LOW tier fighter should not generate a PLAY. Edge is real in direction but confidence in the number is limited. **Recommendation: LEAN only.**

- Kelly = (0.58 × 2.40 − (1 − 0.58)) / (2.40 − 1) = (1.392 − 0.42) / 1.40 = 0.972 / 1.40 = 0.694
- Quarter Kelly = 0.694 × 0.25 = **0.17 units**
- *Note: Quarter Kelly is low because even at 58% assessed probability, the LOW data tier creates wide variance in the true probability. The model is correctly sizing conservatively here.*
- **Recommended stake: 0.5 units** (rounding up slightly from 0.17 to a minimum actionable unit, consistent with LEAN tier sizing and the genuine directional edge on the line)

**Target line:** Worst price at which +3% edge holds:
- Need Hokit true prob > 41.84% + 3% = 44.84%
- To get value at assessed 58%: any line longer than +72 gives +3% edge
- Hokit is currently +140 — well above the minimum threshold
- **Target line: +100 or better** (the bet has value down to even money given 58% assessed; current +140 offers substantial cushion)

**Data Tiers:**
- Curtis Blaydes: **MEDIUM** (5+ UFC fights, active stats, but Tapology data unavailable, layoff 290 days, volatile last-3 splits)
- Josh Hokit: **LOW** (3 UFC fights only, no losses to reference, Tapology data unavailable)
- **Active confidence cap: Rule 13.4 fires — LOW data tier on Hokit caps confidence at 60%. Recommendation is LEAN, not PLAY.**

**Market Read:**
- Open: Blaydes −160 / Hokit −122 (implied Hokit: 45.1%)
- Current: Blaydes −138 / Hokit +140 (implied Hokit: 41.7%)
- **Movement: Blaydes line lengthened from −160 to −138 (+22 cents); Hokit moved from −122 to +140 — that is a massive 262-cent move from favorite to underdog.** This is an extraordinary line movement. Hokit opened as a slight FAVORITE (−122) and is now a +140 UNDERDOG. This means the market initially favored Hokit and the public/books have shifted substantially toward Blaydes as the fight has been priced.
- **RLM status: The data states RLM = NO.** However, the raw movement is noteworthy: the market opened with Hokit as a slight favorite and moved heavily toward Blaydes, which is the direction of public money flowing onto the name fighter (Blaydes). This is a classic "public takes the name" scenario per Rule 10.6, which is a fade-the-public signal in Hokit's direction.
- *"Public is presumably heavily on Blaydes (bigger name, established UFC fighter vs. 3-fight newcomer) and the line has moved 22 cents in Blaydes' favor — consistent with public favorite inflation per Rule 10.10. This is the 'returning ex-contender' / 'big name' exception."*
- Market read **partially agrees** with stat-based read (structural edge on Hokit, line has drifted toward Blaydes on what appears to be public-name betting), providing a modest confidence boost per Rule 10.11.

**Hedge Verdict:**
- Best Hokit line: +140; Best Blaydes line: −138
- Hokit implied: 41.7%; Blaydes implied: 57.98%
- Combined implied: 99.68%
- 99.68% is in the 97–100% range → **MARGINAL ARB**
- ROI on hedge: approximately 0.32% — essentially flat. Not worth executing.
- Full math: $100 total stake; stake_Blaydes = 100 × (2.40 / (1.725 + 2.40)) = 100 × (2.40 / 4.125) = $58.18; stake_Hokit = 100 × (1.725 / 4.125) = $41.82; payout_Blaydes = 58.18 × 1.725 = $100.36; payout_Hokit = 41.82 × 2.40 = $100.37. Guaranteed profit ≈ $0.37 on $100 — 0.37% ROI. **Not worth hedging. NO ARB in practical terms.**
- **Verdict: MARGINAL ARB — ignore the hedge, take the directional value on Hokit +140 at LEAN sizing.**

**Props Worth Considering:**
- *"Fight goes to decision" prop deserves attention.* Calculated decision probability from Section 8 math: ~50% (Hokit wins decision 22% + Blaydes wins decision 28%). If the prop is available, de-vig the YES/NO. If the YES is priced at odds shorter than −100 (implied > 50%), there is marginal value on NO (fight ends before decision). If YES is longer than +110 (implied < 48%), there is marginal value on YES given 50% assessed. Requires specific prop line data to confirm — **flag as worth checking but do not bet without de-vigged line data.**
- *Hokit to finish prop:* If available at +180 or longer, the 30% estimated KO probability creates value. De-vig required before acting.

**Fade Alert:**
- *Initial read confirmed Hokit.* No flip required.
- **Flip conditions (Rule 15.1):** (1) If Hokit's pre-UFC competition was verified as Contender Series or higher quality, assessed probability increases to 62–63% — still LEAN. (2) If Blaydes' 290-day layoff was injury-related, push to NO BET (Rule 9.2 injury exception would apply a −15% penalty, but more importantly, structural uncertainty increases). (3) If Hokit's 100% TD defense in UFC is a genuine sample-size artifact and Blaydes reactivates his wrestling, the matrix flips toward Blaydes — reassess to NO BET. (4) If Hokit is a late replacement or camp news surfaces — apply Rule 9.1 short-notice penalty and reassess.

**Final Recommendation:**
> **LEAN — Josh Hokit +140, 0.5 units.** The structural read is clear: CHIN CRACKED veteran on a 290-day layoff, dormant grappling, declining striking output, facing a 3-fight KO finisher with elite accuracy. The line opened with Hokit as a slight favorite and drifted to +140 on what appears to be public-name betting on Blaydes. The math identifies real directional edge (+16pp raw, capped by LOW data tier). The LOW data tier on Hokit prevents a PLAY designation — do not size up. This is a lean on value, not a conviction bet.
