Two of the three came in Round 1, and one (Pavlovich) came from a non-elite finisher by historical standards. Criteria from Rule 6.2 clearly met: 2+ KO losses in last 3 years, most recent KO in R1, and he is 35 years old (heavyweight age cliff triggers at 37 but chin decay at 35+ is real). **Flag: CHIN CRACKED.**
- **Age cliff at 35 (HW)** — Rule 9.7 places the heavyweight cliff at 37, meaning the formal penalty does not yet fire. However, the chin-crack pattern at 35 is behaviorally equivalent. The Aspinall rematch (R1 KO) and Pavlovich loss (R1 KO) are concerning patterns regardless of the formal cliff threshold.
- **Stat-trend analysis — CAUTION FLAGS:** SLpM last-3 average: (0.8 + 5.0 + 0.0) / 3 = **1.93 vs. career 3.56 — TRENDING DOWN (−46%)**. TD/15 last-3: (2.0 + 0.0 + 0.0) / 3 = **0.67 vs. career 5.38 — TRENDING DOWN (−88%)**. The grappling output has essentially vanished. Str. Acc. last-3 (excluding the 0% Almeida fight which was a 36-second blitz): roughly 49% — near flat. SApM data too variable to trend cleanly, but absorbing a R1 KO from Aspinall is a significant data point.
- **Layoff: 290 days** — Falls in the 270–365-day bucket (Rule 9.2): **−6% win probability penalty**. Fight against Kuniev (June 2025) was a split decision win, suggesting he was not at peak form coming off prior inactivity.

<!--END-->

<!--F2_PROFILE-->
## Josh Hokit -- Style & Profile

- **Data Tier: LOW** — Only 3 UFC fights, all wins, no losses to reference for durability. Pre-UFC record is 5-0 but opponent quality is unverifiable as regional/Contender Series level. All analysis carries LOW-tier confidence cap: **maximum 60% assessed probability (LEAN only, no PLAY).**
- **Archetype: Pressure Wrestler / Volume Striker (FORWARD)** — Career SLpM of 6.07 with 66% striking accuracy is elite output. TD/15 of 8.74 is extremely high. However, last-3 splits show wide variance: SLpM ranged from 0.66 to 9.64, suggesting his style adapts heavily to opponent resistance. He appears to prefer striking but will shoot heavily when striking is working (Uriel fight: 12.3 TD/15, Gimenis fight: 0 TDs but 9.64 SLpM KO).
- **100% TD defense in UFC** — Three fights, zero successful takedowns allowed. This is a meaningful early signal but the sample (3 fights) is too small to declare it career-defining. Treat as notable but not fully reliable.
- **Finishing ability is striking-based, not submission-based** — 3 KO/TKO wins, 0 submissions in UFC career. Sub Avg. is 0.0. Despite elite grappling volume, he uses takedowns as setup for ground-and-pound, not for submission hunting. Secondary tendency: clinch wrestling to top control, finish by strikes.
- **Opponent quality — DISCOUNT REQUIRED** — Guilherme Uriel, Max Gimenis, Denzel Freeman are all unranked UFC fighters with limited records. Per Rule 3.2, Hokit's impressive stats (6.07 SLpM, 66% Str. Acc., 8.74 TD/15) must be heavily discounted until tested against meaningful competition. Flag: **"Trending up against unranked opponents — discount heavily."**
- **Fresh, sharp** — 73-day layoff, no weight issues indicated, 28 years old. No intangibles penalties apply. No chin data available (never been in serious trouble in 3 UFC fights).

<!--END-->

<!--HEAD2HEAD-->
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

**Matrix outcome: Pressure Wrestler (Hokit) vs. Pressure Wrestler / Clinch Striker (Blaydes).** Two FORWARD fighters meeting — Rule 4.4 signals to expect an aggressive early pace. The matrix for mirror-archetype pressure wrestlers collapses to the TD defense variable (Rule 5.2). Blaydes' TD defense is listed at 31% career — dramatically below the 60% threshold at which the stat-based edge flips toward the wrestler. Hokit's 8.74 TD/15min career average combined with Blaydes' 31% TD defense creates a structural opening for Hokit to take this fight to the ground at will. However, Hokit's grappling is primarily ground-and-pound oriented, and Blaydes — when healthy and not chinned — is an elite wrestler himself who knows how to recover position. The key variable is whether Blaydes can use his 7-inch reach advantage to keep Hokit at the end of his punches and deny the takedown entries.

**The chin is the decisive variable.** Blaydes has been CHIN CRACKED by two of the better fighters in the division (Aspinall, Pavlovich) plus Derrick Lewis before them — all via strikes. Hokit is a 3-fight KO artist averaging 6.07 SLpM with 66% accuracy. Against a fighter who absorbs KOs in Round 1, this is exactly the structural mismatch that Rule 6.2 was designed to flag. Blaydes' 7-inch reach advantage matters in the abstract, but his increasingly dormant grappling (TD/15 effectively zero in last-3) means he is not using his primary defensive tool (level changes, forward wrestling pressure) to disrupt strikers. The Aspinall R1 KO and Pavlovich R1 KO both followed Blaydes trying to strike from the outside and absorbing clean counters. Hokit's forward pressure style would not give Blaydes the same long-range countering opportunity he typically prefers.

**Path to victory for Blaydes:** Use the 7-inch reach advantage to keep Hokit at range, land his power right hand (which has been his primary KO weapon historically), and attempt to revive the wrestling game that has been absent in last-3. If he can stuff Hokit's early takedown attempts, the experience gap and quality-of-opponent gap favor a Blaydes decision or late KO. The critical flip point: Blaydes needs to NOT absorb a clean early shot, because the chin data says he does not recover from those anymore. **Path to victory for Hokit:** Press forward immediately, close the reach gap with level changes (Hokit's 31% TD defense on Blaydes means he gets doubles and singles whenever he commits), mix strikes with wrestling to prevent Blaydes from setting his feet, and look for the finish in the first or second round before any experience/craft advantage can manifest. **Net intangible delta: Hokit has a −6% layoff advantage** (Hokit: 0 intangible penalties; Blaydes: −6% for 290-day layoff, plus CHIN CRACKED flag). The data clearly leans toward Hokit as the structural and physical threat — but the LOW data tier on Hokit forces hard confidence caps.

<!--END-->

<!--ENDINGS-->
## 3-5 Most Likely Fight Endings (Ranked by Probability)

**Section 8 math inputs:**
- Hokit base KO rate: 3 KO wins / 8 total fights = 37.5% raw. However, vs. UFC opponents only: 3/3 = 100%, but sample is 3 fights — discount toward ~40% per Rule 8.5 opponent quality concern.
- Blaydes Str. Def.: 58% (career). Chin modifier: 1.4 (CHIN CRACKED). Cardio modifier: 1.0 (unknown rounds, assume 3).
- **Adjusted Hokit KO prob = 0.40 × (1 − 0.58) × 1.4 × 1.0 = 0.40 × 0.42 × 1.4 = 0.235 ≈ 24%**

- Blaydes base KO rate: 4 KO wins / 19 total fights = 21%. Vs. ranked: lower — approximately 15% defensible.
- Hokit Str. Def.: 66% (career, small sample). Chin modifier: 1.0 (no data on chin durability). Cardio modifier: 1.0.
- **Adjusted Blaydes KO prob on Hokit = 0.15 × (1 − 0.66) × 1.0 = 0.15 × 0.34 = 0.051 ≈ 5%**

- Blaydes base TD ability: 5.38 TD/15 career, but last-3 average: 0.67 TD/15 — trend near zero. Hokit TD defense 100% in 3 UFC fights. Sub rate: 0.0. Adjusted submission threat from Blaydes: negligible (< 2%).

- Decision probability = 1 − 0.24 (Hokit KO) − 0.05 (Blaydes KO) − 0.02 (other finishes) = **~69% decision/non-KO**. This seems high given both fighters' finishing tendencies, but reflects Blaydes' durability in decision fights (has gone 3 and 5 rounds multiple times) and the uncertainty of a low-data Hokit. Splitting this into decision for Hokit and decision for Blaydes based on overall stylistic read.

---

**#1 — Josh Hokit wins by KO/TKO, Round 1 or 2**
Probability: 30%
Why: The CHIN CRACKED flag on Blaydes combined with Hokit's 6.07 SLpM and 66% accuracy creates the highest-probability path to a finish. Blaydes has absorbed R1 KOs from Aspinall and Pavlovich — both times when absorbing a sharp counter — and Hokit's forward pressure style will close the reach gap quickly. The 7-inch reach disadvantage does not help a fighter with Blaydes' established chin fragility if he cannot keep Hokit at range for the full fight. Hokit has finished all 3 UFC opponents, two in Round 1.

**#2 — Curtis Blaydes wins by Decision**
Probability: 28%
Why: Blaydes' experience gap (19 fights vs. 8), reach advantage (7 inches), and historical ability to outwork opponents over 15 minutes (3 decision wins in last 10, including a 5-rounder vs. Volkov) give him a legitimate path to outpointing a fighter who has never been tested past Round 2. If Blaydes uses the jab to maintain range, stuffs the early takedown attempts, and avoids the first clean exchange, his craft and durability in non-finish scenarios could get him through 3 rounds. Hokit's opponent quality does not include anyone who can test game-plan execution over a full fight.

**#3 — Josh Hokit wins by Decision**
Probability: 22%
Why: If Hokit cannot finish Blaydes early, his forward pressure and 8.74 TD/15 career average means he can control the fight on the ground through all three rounds. Blaydes' 31% TD defense and near-zero recent grappling output (TD/15 of 0.67 in last-3) mean Hokit can take him down repeatedly. Even without a finish, consistent top control against a shot wrestler at 35 with chin concerns likely wins rounds on the judges' cards.

**#4 — Curtis Blaydes wins by KO/TKO**
Probability: 7%
Why: Blaydes has legitimate power (4 KOs in last 10, including a 15-second R1 KO of Tom Aspinall in their first fight). If the reach advantage allows him to land the right hand before Hokit closes the distance, an early finish is possible. However, the adjusted KO probability of ~5% against an untested chin and Hokit's 100% TD defense in UFC makes this a low-probability path. The experience gap and power history are the only things keeping this scenario on the board.

<!--END-->

<!--BETTING-->

**TIER: LEAN (not a PLAY — LOW data tier cap on Hokit forces maximum 60% assessed probability)**

**Primary Pick: Josh Hokit — Confidence: 58%**

**Edge Breakdown:**
- Line: Josh Hokit +140
- Implied probability: 100 / (140 + 100) = **41.7%**
- De-vig: Blaydes −138 → implied = 138 / (138 + 100) = 57.98%; Hokit +140 → 41.7%; Sum = 99.68%; De-vig true Hokit = 41.7 / 99.68 = **41.84%**
- Assessed probability (Hokit wins): **58%** (structural favorite on matrix, CHIN CRACKED opponent, fresh camp, 290-day Blaydes layoff; capped at 60% due to LOW data tier on Hokit)
- **Edge: 58.0% − 41.84% = +16.16%** — Edge this large triggers Rule 11.3's re-check requirement. Re-examining: The edge is inflated by Hokit's LOW data tier, meaning the 58% assessed probability itself is carrying significant uncertainty. The LOW data cap is already applied (preventing a higher confidence call), but the raw edge number reflects a genuinely mispriced underdog line. Still, a LOW tier fighter should not generate a PLAY. Edge is real in direction but confidence in the number is limited. **Recommendation: LEAN only.**

**Stake: Quarter-Kelly calculation:**
- Decimal odds for Hokit at +140: (140/100) + 1 = 2.40
- Kelly = (0.58 × 2.40 − (1 − 0.58)) / (2.40 − 1) = (1.392 − 0.42) / 1.40 = 0.972 / 1.40 = 0.694
- Quarter Kelly = 0.694 × 0.25 = **0.17 units**
- *Note: Quarter Kelly is low because even at 58% assessed probability, the LOW data tier creates wide variance in the true probability. The model is correctly sizing conservatively here.*
- **Recommended stake: 0.5 units** (rounding up slightly from 0.17 to a minimum actionable unit, consistent with LEAN tier sizing and the genuine directional edge on the line)

**Target line:** Worst price at which +3% edge holds:
- Need Hokit true prob > 41.84% + 3% = 44.84%
- To get value at assessed 58%: any line longer than +72 gives +3% edge
- Hokit is currently +140 — well above the minimum threshold
- **Target line: +100 or better** (the bet has value down to even money given 58% assessed; current +140 offers substantial cushion)

**Data Tiers:**
- Curtis Blaydes: **MEDIUM** (5+ UFC fights, active stats, but Tapology data unavailable, layoff 290 days, volatile last-3 splits)
- Josh Hokit: **LOW** (3 UFC fights only, no losses to reference, Tapology data unavailable)
- **Active confidence cap: Rule 13.4 fires — LOW data tier on Hokit caps confidence at 60%. Recommendation is LEAN, not PLAY.**

**Market Read:**
- Open: Blaydes −160 / Hokit −122 (implied Hokit: 45.1%)
- Current: Blaydes −138 / Hokit +140 (implied Hokit: 41.7%)
- **Movement: Blaydes line lengthened from −160 to −138 (+22 cents); Hokit moved from −122 to +140 — that is a massive 262-cent move from favorite to underdog.** This is an extraordinary line movement. Hokit opened as a slight FAVORITE (−122) and is now a +140 UNDERDOG. This means the market initially favored Hokit and the public/books have shifted substantially toward Blaydes as the fight has been priced.
- **RLM status: The data states RLM = NO.** However, the raw movement is noteworthy: the market opened with Hokit as a slight favorite and moved heavily toward Blaydes, which is the direction of public money flowing onto the name fighter (Blaydes). This is a classic "public takes the name" scenario per Rule 10.6, which is a fade-the-public signal in Hokit's direction.
- *"Public is presumably heavily on Blaydes (bigger name, established UFC fighter vs. 3-fight newcomer) and the line has moved 22 cents in Blaydes' favor — consistent with public favorite inflation per Rule 10.10. This is the 'returning ex-contender' / 'big name' exception."*
- Market read **partially agrees** with stat-based read (structural edge on Hokit, line has drifted toward Blaydes on what appears to be public-name betting), providing a modest confidence boost per Rule 10.11.

**Hedge Verdict:**
- Best Hokit line: +140; Best Blaydes line: −138
- Hokit implied: 41.7%; Blaydes implied: 57.98%
- Combined implied: 99.68%
- 99.68% is in the 97–100% range → **MARGINAL ARB**
- ROI on hedge: approximately 0.32% — essentially flat. Not worth executing.
- Full math: $100 total stake; stake_Blaydes = 100 × (2.40 / (1.725 + 2.40)) = 100 × (2.40 / 4.125) = $58.18; stake_Hokit = 100 × (1.725 / 4.125) = $41.82; payout_Blaydes = 58.18 × 1.725 = $100.36; payout_Hokit = 41.82 × 2.40 = $100.37. Guaranteed profit ≈ $0.37 on $100 — 0.37% ROI. **Not worth hedging. NO ARB in practical terms.**
- **Verdict: MARGINAL ARB — ignore the hedge, take the directional value on Hokit +140 at LEAN sizing.**

**Props Worth Considering:**
- *"Fight goes to decision" prop deserves attention.* Calculated decision probability from Section 8 math: ~50% (Hokit wins decision 22% + Blaydes wins decision 28%). If the prop is available, de-vig the YES/NO. If the YES is priced at odds shorter than −100 (implied > 50%), there is marginal value on NO (fight ends before decision). If YES is longer than +110 (implied < 48%), there is marginal value on YES given 50% assessed. Requires specific prop line data to confirm — **flag as worth checking but do not bet without de-vigged line data.**
- *Hokit to finish prop:* If available at +180 or longer, the 30% estimated KO probability creates value. De-vig required before acting.

**Fade Alert:**
- *Initial read confirmed Hokit.* No flip required.
- **Flip conditions (Rule 15.1):** (1) If Hokit's pre-UFC competition was verified as Contender Series or higher quality, assessed probability increases to 62–63% — still LEAN. (2) If Blaydes' 290-day layoff was injury-related, push to NO BET (Rule 9.2 injury exception would apply a −15% penalty, but more importantly, structural uncertainty increases). (3) If Hokit's 100% TD defense in UFC is a genuine sample-size artifact and Blaydes reactivates his wrestling, the matrix flips toward Blaydes — reassess to NO BET. (4) If Hokit is a late replacement or camp news surfaces — apply Rule 9.1 short-notice penalty and reassess.

**Final Recommendation:**
> **LEAN — Josh Hokit +140, 0.5 units.** The structural read is clear: CHIN CRACKED veteran on a 290-day layoff, dormant grappling, declining striking output, facing a 3-fight KO finisher with elite accuracy. The line opened with Hokit as a slight favorite and drifted to +140 on what appears to be public-name betting on Blaydes. The math identifies real directional edge (+16pp raw, capped by LOW data tier). The LOW data tier on Hokit prevents a PLAY designation — do not size up. This is a lean on value, not a conviction bet.

<!--END-->

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Dominick Reyes -- Style & Profile

- **Data Tier: MEDIUM** (5+ UFC fights, full career stats available, but severe last-3 variance and one core concern: most recent fight is a R1 KO loss, 192-day layoff). Archetype: **Power Striker**. Tag: **FORWARD**.
- **Striking output is trending down sharply**: Career SLpM 5.39, but last-3 averages show 1.12 / 3.33 / 4.91 — the Ulberg fight (1.12 SLpM) represents a near-complete offensive collapse in a R1 KO loss. The most recent fight is the most relevant, and it is an outlier in the wrong direction.
- **CHIN CRACKED alert**: 4 KO/TKO losses in the last 3 years (Prochazka R2, Blachowicz R2, Spann R1, Ulberg R1). Two losses came in R1. Rule 6.2 criteria met: 2+ KO losses in last 3 years ✓, most recent KO loss in R1 ✓, defensive decay (SApM career 3.49, Str. Def. 49% — among the lowest in the division for an active fighter) ✓. **CHIN CRACKED confirmed.**
- **Strike defense (49%) is well below LHW average**: Absorbing 3.49 sig. strikes/min career-wide against a division that regularly features elite strikers. The Ulberg R1 finish confirms the chin is not recovering between fights.
- **Grappling is vestigial**: 0.29 TDs/15min, 28% accuracy. Reyes does not use wrestling offensively or defensively as a style element. He is entirely a stand-up fighter — this fight will be decided on the feet, period.
- **Intangibles**: Age 36, men's LHW — Rule 9.7 age cliff fires at 35 for this division. Penalty: −5% × 1 year past 35 = **−5%**. Returning from R1 KO loss (Ulberg) — Rule 9.6 applies: **−10% mental discount**. **Do not double-count with layoff** (192 days = −6% per Rule 9.2, but the KO-return penalty at −10% is harsher — apply KO-return penalty only: **−10%**). Net intangible penalty for Reyes: **−15%**.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Johnny Walker -- Style & Profile

- **Data Tier: MEDIUM** (sufficient UFC fights, but 2 core concerns: last-3 splits are highly inconsistent across fight contexts, and career TDD of 57% is a structural vulnerability). Archetype: **Power Striker** with a secondary of clinch/dirty boxing (long frame, 82" reach). Tag: **FORWARD**.
- **Last-3 SLpM is all over the place**: 1.18 (vs. Mingyang, short 2-round fight) / 6.08 (vs. Oezdemir, fight that ended in a R1 KO loss) / 1.95 (vs. Ankalaev). Career SLpM 4.06. Trend: **FLAT to DOWN** against quality opposition. The Mingyang win (1.18 SLpM) inflates his "win column" against a fighter ranked below 20.
- **Strike defense at 45% is alarming**: Career-wide Walker absorbs 3.09 sig. strikes/min at 45% Str. Def. — he gets hit frequently. This is partly a style artifact (forward pressure, trades at close range), but it creates CHIN CRACKED risk. Rule 6.2 check: 2+ KO losses in last 3 years (Oezdemir R1, Ankalaev R2, Hill R1 — 3 KO losses in last ~3 years) ✓, most recent KO loss in R1 (Oezdemir) ✓. **CHIN CRACKED confirmed for Walker as well.**
- **Reach advantage is his most important asset**: 82" reach vs. Reyes' 77" — a **5-inch gap** (Rule 5.3 threshold: ≥4" = real edge). Walker can fire at distance that Reyes cannot comfortably return from, especially relevant if Reyes' offensive output remains suppressed.
- **Takedown defense (57%) is a liability**: Below the critical 60% threshold from Rule 5.2 — but with Reyes at 0.29 TDs/15min and 28% accuracy, this hole will never be exposed. This stat is irrelevant in this matchup.
- **Intangibles**: Age 34, layoff 227 days. Rule 9.2: 180–270 days = **−3%**. Returning from a R1 KO loss (Oezdemir, Jun 2024) — he won vs. Mingyang since, so the KO-return penalty does not apply to this fight. No confirmed short notice. Net intangible penalty for Walker: **−3%** (layoff only).
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

This is a **FORWARD vs. FORWARD** power striker matchup between two CHIN CRACKED fighters — the highest-variance fight archetype in MMA. Per the style matrix (Rule 5.1: Power Striker vs. Power Striker), the outcome defaults to "whoever lands first," and the structural edge shifts decisively to whoever has the longer reach, cleaner defense, and more durable chin. On all three criteria, Walker holds a narrow but real lead: 82" vs. 77" reach, career SApM of 3.09 vs. Reyes' 3.49, and importantly, Walker has a post-KO-loss win on his record (Mingyang) while Reyes is walking into this fight directly off a R1 KO loss to Ulberg with no bounce-back fight in between.

The master variable here is not TDD (both fighters almost never shoot), but rather **whose chin holds longer under power shots**. Reyes' chin profile is materially more concerning: 4 KO/TKO losses in ~3 years, with R1 finishes from Spann (a non-elite striker, Sub rule 6.2 criterion 3 nearly met) and Ulberg (who is dangerous but not an elite KO artist). His strike defense has been 49% career-wide and he's absorbing shots at a high clip. Walker's chin is also cracked, but he's coming in off a win and has one more recovery fight under his belt. The 5-inch reach advantage is decisive for a match like this — it means Walker controls the exchanges at the range where Reyes cannot comfortably land his southpaw left hand. Reyes needs to close distance to score, and every step forward puts his cracked chin into danger territory.

The **net intangible delta** strongly favors Walker: Reyes is carrying a −15% penalty (age cliff + KO-return discount) against Walker's −3% (layoff only). That's a 12-percentage-point swing purely from intangibles, before the stylistic read even factors in. Walker's path to victory is straightforward: use his reach, keep the fight at his preferred distance, and land one clean power shot on a fighter whose chin can no longer absorb elite contact. Reyes' path requires early pressure, cutting the distance before Walker can establish his jab, and landing the southpaw left hand first — the same blueprint that worked against Krylov and Smith but completely failed against Ulberg. That failure was only 192 days ago.

**Flip conditions (Rule 15.1):** If Reyes' Ulberg loss was an anomaly caused by a specific technical breakdown that has since been corrected in camp (no data available), switch to NO BET. If Walker arrives visibly weight-drained or underweight at weigh-ins (late line movement would signal this), push to NO BET. If scheduled rounds are confirmed as 5, the cardio dynamic changes — assess both fighters' R3+ history before betting.
analysis_sections.endings:
## 3-5 Most Likely Fight Endings (Ranked by Probability)

**Base KO math for Walker finishing Reyes:**
- Walker career KO rate: 3 KO wins / last 10 = 30% as a baseline. Career-wide: ~15 KO wins / 22 total = 68% raw, but vs. ranked opposition the rate is lower. Use 45% as adjusted base.
- Reyes Str. Def: 49% → (1 - 0.49) = 0.51
- chin_modifier: CHIN CRACKED = 1.4
- adjusted_KO_prob = 0.45 × 0.51 × 1.4 = **32.1%**

**Base KO math for Reyes finishing Walker:**
- Reyes career KO rate: 4 KO / last 10 fights = 40% baseline, adjusted for quality ~35%
- Walker Str. Def: 45% → (1 - 0.45) = 0.55
- chin_modifier: CHIN CRACKED = 1.4
- adjusted_KO_prob = 0.35 × 0.55 × 1.4 = **26.9%**

**Decision probability (Section 8.4):**
decision_prob ≈ 1 - 0.321 - 0.269 = **41%** — but both fighters have nearly zero decision history in recent years; discount decision likelihood to ~20%.
Redistributing: Walker KO ~36%, Reyes KO ~31%, Decision ~18%, other ~15%.

---

**#1 — Johnny Walker wins by KO/TKO, Round 1 or 2**
Probability: 36%
Why: Walker's 5-inch reach advantage lets him initiate exchanges at the range where Reyes cannot comfortably answer. Reyes, coming off a R1 KO loss and carrying CHIN CRACKED status, needs to close distance to land his southpaw left — every time he does so he eats power shots from a longer fighter. Walker's recent R2 KO finish of Mingyang shows the pattern still works when he controls range. This is the most structurally supported outcome.

**#2 — Dominick Reyes wins by KO/TKO, Round 1**
Probability: 31%
Why: Reyes has genuine first-round finishing power (KO wins vs. Krylov R1, Jacoby R1, Weidman R1, Smith R2) and the southpaw stance creates real angle problems for Walker who has faced primarily orthodox fighters. If Reyes is able to land first and stun Walker early, Walker's own CHIN CRACKED status means the fight ends quickly. The entire probability mass for Reyes is concentrated in the first two rounds — he cannot sustain output long-term, as shown by the Ulberg implosion.

**#3 — Fight goes to decision**
Probability: 18%
Why: Both fighters are capable of extended bouts but both have cracked chins in a division full of power. Decision_prob formula gives ~41% raw, but fight history for both fighters trends heavily toward stoppages (Reyes: 4 KO wins, 4 KO losses in last 10; Walker: 3 KO wins, 3 KO losses in last 5). The base formula overstates decisions here. Adjusted to 18% — only likely if neither lands cleanly and both fighters box carefully from distance for 3 rounds.

**#4 — Dominick Reyes wins by KO/TKO, Round 2 or 3**
Probability: 9%
Why: If Reyes survives early exchanges and Walker begins to tire (Walker's SLpM cratered to 1.95 against Ankalaev in a grind fight), Reyes' power re-emerges. Historically Reyes was more active in later rounds, though this pattern predates his chin issues. Low probability given his current chin concerns, but not zero.
analysis_sections.betting:
## Betting Recommendation

**1. Tier Label: PLAY**

**2. Primary Pick: Johnny Walker — Confidence: 62%**

**3. Edge Breakdown:**
- Line: Walker +130 (current)
- Implied probability: 100 / (130 + 100) = **43.5%**
- De-vig: Reyes −140 → 140/240 = 58.3%; Walker +130 → 43.5%; Sum = 101.8%
- De-vigged true prob Walker: 43.5 / 101.8 = **42.7%**
- Assessed probability for Walker: **62%**
  - Matrix baseline (Power vs. Power, Walker has reach + cleaner chin trajectory): ~52%
  - Net intangible delta: Walker +12pp over Reyes (Reyes −15%, Walker −3%)
  - Final assessed: 52% + 10% (intangible adjustment, capped conservatively) = **62%**
- **Edge: 62% − 42.7% = +19.3pp**

⚠️ **REVIEW FLAG (Rule 13.2):** Edge above 12% — re-check work per ruleset. Re-examined: the large edge is driven by (a) Walker at +130 being a dog when he likely should be a slight favorite, (b) Reyes carrying confirmed CHIN CRACKED status walking into this fight directly off a R1 KO loss with no bounce-back, and (c) a 5-inch reach advantage. The line appears to be overweighting Reyes' name recognition and three-fight win streak (vs. Smith/Jacoby/Krylov) without adequately adjusting for the Ulberg R1 KO loss and age cliff. Market movement (Reyes lengthened from −150 to −140, Walker from −127 to +130) shows both sides moved away from their opening — this is unusual and suggests the book is uncertain. RLM is NOT flagged, but the line drift toward neither side is a muted cautionary signal. After re-check: the edge appears genuine, not an error. Data tier for Walker is MEDIUM → **confidence capped at 67%** (Rule 13.4). Revised confidence: **62%** (within cap — stands as PLAY).

**4. Stake Recommendation:**
- Walker decimal odds: (130/100) + 1 = 2.30
- kelly_fraction = (0.193 × 2.30 − (1 − 0.62)) / (2.30 − 1)
- kelly_fraction = (0.4439 − 0.38) / 1.30 = 0.0639 / 1.30 = **0.0492 (full Kelly)**
- Quarter Kelly: 0.0492 × 0.25 = **1.2% of bankroll → 1.2 units**
- Capped at 3 units (no issue here)

<!--F1_PROFILE-->
## Dominick Reyes -- Style & Profile

- **Data Tier: MEDIUM** (5+ UFC fights, full career stats available, but severe last-3 variance and one core concern: most recent fight is a R1 KO loss, 192-day layoff). Archetype: **Power Striker**. Tag: **FORWARD**.
- **Striking output is trending down sharply**: Career SLpM 5.39, but last-3 averages show 1.12 / 3.33 / 4.91 — the Ulberg fight (1.12 SLpM) represents a near-complete offensive collapse in a R1 KO loss. The most recent fight is the most relevant, and it is an outlier in the wrong direction.
- **CHIN CRACKED alert**: 4 KO/TKO losses in the last 3 years (Prochazka R2, Blachowicz R2, Spann R1, Ulberg R1). Two losses came in R1. Rule 6.2 criteria met: 2+ KO losses in last 3 years ✓, most recent KO loss in R1 ✓, defensive decay (SApM career 3.49, Str. Def. 49% — among the lowest in the division for an active fighter) ✓. **CHIN CRACKED confirmed.**
- **Strike defense (49%) is well below LHW average**: Absorbing 3.49 sig. strikes/min career-wide against a division that regularly features elite strikers. The Ulberg R1 finish confirms the chin is not recovering between fights.
- **Grappling is vestigial**: 0.29 TDs/15min, 28% accuracy. Reyes does not use wrestling offensively or defensively as a style element. He is entirely a stand-up fighter — this fight will be decided on the feet, period.
- **Intangibles**: Age 36, men's LHW — Rule 9.7 age cliff fires at 35 for this division. Penalty: −5% × 1 year past 35 = **−5%**. Returning from R1 KO loss (Ulberg) — Rule 9.6 applies: **−10% mental discount**. **Do not double-count with layoff** (192 days = −6% per Rule 9.2, but the KO-return penalty at −10% is harsher — apply KO-return penalty only: **−10%**). Net intangible penalty for Reyes: **−15%**.
<!--END-->

<!--F2_PROFILE-->
## Johnny Walker -- Style & Profile

- **Data Tier: MEDIUM** (sufficient UFC fights, but 2 core concerns: last-3 splits are highly inconsistent across fight contexts, and career TDD of 57% is a structural vulnerability). Archetype: **Power Striker** with a secondary of clinch/dirty boxing (long frame, 82" reach). Tag: **FORWARD**.
- **Last-3 SLpM is all over the place**: 1.18 (vs. Mingyang, short 2-round fight) / 6.08 (vs. Oezdemir, fight that ended in a R1 KO loss) / 1.95 (vs. Ankalaev). Career SLpM 4.06. Trend: **FLAT to DOWN** against quality opposition. The Mingyang win (1.18 SLpM) inflates his "win column" against a fighter ranked below 20.
- **Strike defense at 45% is alarming**: Career-wide Walker absorbs 3.09 sig. strikes/min at 45% Str. Def. — he gets hit frequently. This is partly a style artifact (forward pressure, trades at close range), but it creates CHIN CRACKED risk. Rule 6.2 check: 2+ KO losses in last 3 years (Oezdemir R1, Ankalaev R2, Hill R1 — 3 KO losses in last ~3 years) ✓, most recent KO loss in R1 (Oezdemir) ✓. **CHIN CRACKED confirmed for Walker as well.**
- **Reach advantage is his most important asset**: 82" reach vs. Reyes' 77" — a **5-inch gap** (Rule 5.3 threshold: ≥4" = real edge). Walker can fire at distance that Reyes cannot comfortably return from, especially relevant if Reyes' offensive output remains suppressed.
- **Takedown defense (57%) is a liability**: Below the critical 60% threshold from Rule 5.2 — but with Reyes at 0.29 TDs/15min and 28% accuracy, this hole will never be exposed. This stat is irrelevant in this matchup.
- **Intangibles**: Age 34, layoff 227 days. Rule 9.2: 180–270 days = **−3%**. Returning from a R1 KO loss (Oezdemir, Jun 2024) — he won vs. Mingyang since, so the KO-return penalty does not apply to this fight. No confirmed short notice. Net intangible penalty for Walker: **−3%** (layoff only).
<!--END-->

<!--HEAD2HEAD-->
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

This is a **FORWARD vs. FORWARD** power striker matchup between two CHIN CRACKED fighters — the highest-variance fight archetype in MMA. Per the style matrix (Rule 5.1: Power Striker vs. Power Striker), the outcome defaults to "whoever lands first," and the structural edge shifts decisively to whoever has the longer reach, cleaner defense, and more durable chin. On all three criteria, Walker holds a narrow but real lead: 82" vs. 77" reach, career SApM of 3.09 vs. Reyes' 3.49, and importantly, Walker has a post-KO-loss win on his record (Mingyang) while Reyes is walking into this fight directly off a R1 KO loss to Ulberg with no bounce-back fight in between.

The master variable here is not TDD (both fighters almost never shoot), but rather **whose chin holds longer under power shots**. Reyes' chin profile is materially more concerning: 4 KO/TKO losses in ~3 years, with R1 finishes from Spann (a non-elite striker, Sub rule 6.2 criterion 3 nearly met) and Ulberg (who is dangerous but not an elite KO artist). His strike defense has been 49% career-wide and he's absorbing shots at a high clip. Walker's chin is also cracked, but he's coming in off a win and has one more recovery fight under his belt. The 5-inch reach advantage is decisive for a match like this — it means Walker controls the exchanges at the range where Reyes cannot comfortably land his southpaw left hand. Reyes needs to close distance to score, and every step forward puts his cracked chin into danger territory.

The **net intangible delta** strongly favors Walker: Reyes is carrying a −15% penalty (age cliff + KO-return discount) against Walker's −3% (layoff only). That's a 12-percentage-point swing purely from intangibles, before the stylistic read even factors in. Walker's path to victory is straightforward: use his reach, keep the fight at his preferred distance, and land one clean power shot on a fighter whose chin can no longer absorb elite contact. Reyes' path requires early pressure, cutting the distance before Walker can establish his jab, and landing the southpaw left hand first — the same blueprint that worked against Krylov and Smith but completely failed against Ulberg. That failure was only 192 days ago.

**Flip conditions (Rule 15.1):** If Reyes' Ulberg loss was an anomaly caused by a specific technical breakdown that has since been corrected in camp (no data available), switch to NO BET. If Walker arrives visibly weight-drained or underweight at weigh-ins (late line movement would signal this), push to NO BET. If scheduled rounds are confirmed as 5, the cardio dynamic changes — assess both fighters' R3+ history before betting.
<!--END-->

<!--ENDINGS-->
## 3-5 Most Likely Fight Endings (Ranked by Probability)

**Base KO math for Walker finishing Reyes:**
- Walker career KO rate: 3 KO wins / last 10 = 30% as a baseline. Career-wide: ~15 KO wins / 22 total = 68% raw, but vs. ranked opposition the rate is lower. Use 45% as adjusted base.
- Reyes Str. Def: 49% → (1 - 0.49) = 0.51
- chin_modifier: CHIN CRACKED = 1.4
- adjusted_KO_prob = 0.45 × 0.51 × 1.4 = **32.1%**

**Base KO math for Reyes finishing Walker:**
- Reyes career KO rate: 4 KO / last 10 fights = 40% baseline, adjusted for quality ~35%
- Walker Str. Def: 45% → (1 - 0.45) = 0.55
- chin_modifier: CHIN CRACKED = 1.4
- adjusted_KO_prob = 0.35 × 0.55 × 1.4 = **26.9%**

**Decision probability (Section 8.4):**
decision_prob ≈ 1 - 0.321 - 0.269 = **41%** — but both fighters have nearly zero decision history in recent years; discount decision likelihood to ~20%.
Redistributing: Walker KO ~36%, Reyes KO ~31%, Decision ~18%, other ~15%.

---

**#1 — Johnny Walker wins by KO/TKO, Round 1 or 2**
Probability: 36%
Why: Walker's 5-inch reach advantage lets him initiate exchanges at the range where Reyes cannot comfortably answer. Reyes, coming off a R1 KO loss and carrying CHIN CRACKED status, needs to close distance to land his southpaw left — every time he does so he eats power shots from a longer fighter. Walker's recent R2 KO finish of Mingyang shows the pattern still works when he controls range. This is the most structurally supported outcome.

**#2 — Dominick Reyes wins by KO/TKO, Round 1**
Probability: 31%
Why: Reyes has genuine first-round finishing power (KO wins vs. Krylov R1, Jacoby R1, Weidman R1, Smith R2) and the southpaw stance creates real angle problems for Walker who has faced primarily orthodox fighters. If Reyes is able to land first and stun Walker early, Walker's own CHIN CRACKED status means the fight ends quickly. The entire probability mass for Reyes is concentrated in the first two rounds — he cannot sustain output long-term, as shown by the Ulberg implosion.

**#3 — Fight goes to decision**
Probability: 18%
Why: Both fighters are capable of extended bouts but both have cracked chins in a division full of power. Decision_prob formula gives ~41% raw, but fight history for both fighters trends heavily toward stoppages (Reyes: 4 KO wins, 4 KO losses in last 10; Walker: 3 KO wins, 3 KO losses in last 5). The base formula overstates decisions here. Adjusted to 18% — only likely if neither lands cleanly and both fighters box carefully from distance for 3 rounds.

**#4 — Dominick Reyes wins by KO/TKO, Round 2 or 3**
Probability: 9%
Why: If Reyes survives early exchanges and Walker begins to tire (Walker's SLpM cratered to 1.95 against Ankalaev in a grind fight), Reyes' power re-emerges. Historically Reyes was more active in later rounds, though this pattern predates his chin issues. Low probability given his current chin concerns, but not zero.
<!--END-->

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Cub Swanson -- Style & Profile

- **Data Tier: MEDIUM** (5+ UFC fights, active stats, but 479-day layoff makes recent data suspect per Rule 2.3/3.6). Archetype: **Power/Counter-Striker**. Tag: **BACKWARD** — Swanson wins by picking his spots, not initiating volume exchanges. Career SLpM 4.79 with 51% accuracy, but last-3 SLpM averages only 2.07/min — TRENDING DOWN sharply (-57% vs. career). Volume has collapsed.

- **CHIN CRACKED — flagged.** KO losses: Giga Chikadze R1 (1:03), Jonathan Martinez R2 (4:19). Two KO losses in last 3 years, one in R1, one in R2. Age 42 (5+ years past the 37-cliff for men in lower weight classes). Defensive decay partially confirmed: career SApM 4.00, but Swanson absorbed shots from a R1 KO (Chikadze) and R2 KO (Martinez). The Quarantillo KO win masks a fighter who is routinely getting hurt. Rule 6.2 criteria: ✅ 2+ KO losses in last 3 years, ✅ R1 KO loss (Chikadze), ✅ Age 42 (cliff condition).

- **Age cliff penalty fires hard.** At 42 in the featherweight division (cliff = 35), that's 7 years past cliff. Rule 9.7: −5% per year, capped at −15%. Full cap applied. His win over Quarantillo was real (R3 KO, 68.4% accuracy in that fight — career-high accuracy), but at 42 with 479 days off, that performance is the outlier, not the baseline.

- **Layoff: 479 days.** Rule 9.2: 365–540 days = −10% penalty AND career stats treated as suspect. Swanson's last fight was December 2024; this is a serious comeback timeline at age 42. No reported injury context available to adjust the penalty.

- **Striking accuracy trending interestingly:** last-3 Str. Acc. averages (68.4%, 64.5%, 54.8%) are all ABOVE career 51% — suggesting he's throwing less (SLpM collapsed) but landing at higher accuracy when he does throw. Classic counter-striker aging pattern: he's become more selective, not more effective. This works until he gets hit by someone who punishes hesitancy.

- **Grappling is effectively non-existent in recent fights.** Zero takedown attempts in all three last-3 fights. Career TD avg 1.01/15min is hollow — he hasn't used the grappling dimension in 18+ months of documented fights.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Nate Landwehr -- Style & Profile

- **Data Tier: MEDIUM** (sufficient UFC fights, 269-day layoff within acceptable range, but recent fight data raises serious concerns). Archetype: **Volume Striker / Brawler**. Tag: **FORWARD** — Landwehr initiates, walks forward, and seeks exchanges. Career SLpM 5.63 places him firmly in the volume striker tier, but critically he absorbs 5.83/min — one of the worst SApM rates at featherweight.

- **CHIN CRACKED — flagged.** Two consecutive KO losses: Dooho Choi R3 (Dec 2024) and Morgan Charriere R3 (Jul 2025). Both in Round 3 — this is technically a **cardio-related KO pattern** (Rule 6.4: late stoppages = cardio problem), BUT the back-to-back nature combined with his career-high SApM (5.83) and strike defense trending: career Str. Def. 52% (already low) with last-3 accuracy collapsing to 39.1% and 36.0% — these are deterioration signals. Rule 6.2 criteria: ✅ 2+ KO losses in last 3 years, ✅ SApM career 5.83 (defensive decay signal), ✅ Age 37 (at the cliff for featherweight men). CHIN CRACKED confirmed with cardio overlay.

- **Striking stats in free fall.** Last-3 SLpM: 7.84 (vs. Emmers, WIN), 0.67 (vs. Choi, LOSS), 1.72 (vs. Charriere, LOSS). The Emmers outlier flatters the average. In his two most recent fights (both losses), he landed 0.67 and 1.72/min against a career average of 5.63 — TRENDING DOWN severely in meaningful fights. Str. Acc.: 39.1% and 36.0% in last two — well below career 45%. The "volume striker" identity is eroding in real competition.

- **R3 CARDIO RISK — flagged.** Both recent KO losses came in Round 3. Pattern: Landwehr starts fights forward and active (7.84 vs. Emmers in a short fight), but fades badly as fights progress. Career high SApM suggests he takes punishment early and pays for it late. Rule 7.1: 2+ R3+ losses in last 5 fights = R3 CARDIO RISK explicitly fired.

- **Grappling: low volume but functional.** Career Sub. Avg. 0.8/15min with 2 submission wins in last 10 fights (Klein R3, Lingo R2). TD Def. 71% is respectable. He won't be a grappling force but can threaten from scrambles if the fight goes to the mat. However: zero takedown attempts in each of the last 3 fights — the grappling dimension has also gone dormant.

- **Age cliff: 37, exactly at the featherweight cliff.** Rule 9.7: −5% penalty at cliff initiation. Combined with R3 CARDIO RISK and CHIN CRACKED, this is a fighter whose physical tools are declining on multiple axes simultaneously.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

This matchup pits two CHIN CRACKED, aging featherweights against each other in what the matrix identifies as a **FORWARD (Landwehr) vs. BACKWARD (Swanson)** fight. Per Rule 4.4: the FORWARD fighter (Landwehr) controls cage and output, while the BACKWARD fighter (Swanson) wins exchanges. The structural question becomes: which fighter can execute their identity for more than one round at age 42 and 37, respectively, when both have documented deterioration? The matrix says the BACKWARD counter-striker wins the *exchanges* — but Swanson hasn't consistently executed counter-striking in recent fights. His last-3 SLpM (2.07 average) is barely above Landwehr's collapsed recent numbers (1.72 and 0.67). Neither fighter is functioning anywhere near their career archetype identity.

The critical exchange is simple: Landwehr's opening-round pressure vs. Swanson's counter-striking windows. Landwehr generated 7.84 SLpM against Emmers in a short finish — when he's on, he's on. Against Swanson's 59% career strike defense (already mediocre) and CHIN CRACKED status at 42, Landwehr's pressure *should* generate early openings. However, Landwehr's own defense is catastrophic — 52% career strike defense, and Swanson's counter-striking accuracy has paradoxically *improved* in recent fights (54–68% accuracy) even as volume collapsed. A single clean counter from Swanson — who has 3 KO wins in last 10 fights — could end matters. The key variable is **whether Landwehr can sustain pressure without fading to a point where Swanson's counters find him open**. Given Landwehr's R3 CARDIO RISK and his last two fights ending in R3 stoppages, the answer leans toward "probably not for a full 3 rounds."

Net intangible delta favors **Swanson slightly, perversely**: Swanson's 479-day layoff (−10%) and age cliff (−15%, capped) are brutal, but Landwehr's R3 CARDIO RISK, consecutive KO losses (Rule 6.3: −10% for returning from KO loss), age cliff (−5%), and his own chin deterioration produce a similar or worse intangible stack. Swanson: −25% total (layoff −10%, age cliff capped −15%). Landwehr: −20% estimated (age −5%, returning from KO loss −10%, R3 cardio risk modifier). Both fighters are operating at significantly reduced win probability from their "peak" archetypes — the fight becomes a question of who deteriorates *less*, not who performs best.

**Flip conditions (Rule 15.1):** If Landwehr's R3 losses were primarily due to opponent-specific factors (e.g., Choi's power specifically, Charriere's reach advantage) rather than systemic cardio collapse, downgrade the cardio risk and lean harder to Landwehr. If Swanson's 479-day layoff included a disclosed injury, push to NO BET. If scheduled rounds are 5 (not 3), switch to heavy Swanson fade — his chin and age make 5 rounds untenable.
analysis_sections.endings:
## 3-5 Most Likely Fight Endings (Ranked by Probability)

**Section 8 math baseline:**
- Swanson KO base rate: 3 KOs in last 10 = 30% career-recent rate. Adjusted: 0.30 × (1 − 0.52 Landwehr Str. Def.) × 1.4 (Landwehr CHIN CRACKED) × 1.0 = 0.30 × 0.48 × 1.4 = **~20% adjusted KO prob for Swanson**
- Landwehr KO base rate: 1 KO in last 10 = 10% (his wins are submissions and decisions recently). Career KO % is misleadingly low in last 10. Using 25% career rate adjusted: 0.25 × (1 − 0.59 Swanson Str. Def.) × 1.4 (Swanson CHIN CRACKED) × 1.0 = 0.25 × 0.41 × 1.4 = **~14% adjusted KO prob for Landwehr**
- Sub prob both fighters: near zero given 0 TD attempts in each fighter's last 3 fights.
- Decision prob: 1 − 0.20 − 0.14 − ~0.02 (subs both) ≈ **~64% decision probability baseline**. This feels high given both fighters' finish history, so we'll redistribute some to rounds-specific finishes.

**#1 — Cub Swanson wins by KO/TKO, Round 2 or 3**
Probability: 28%
Why: Swanson's counter-striking accuracy has been 54–68% in recent fights, the highest of his career — he's landing cleaner than ever when he throws. Landwehr's chin is cracked after back-to-back R3 losses, his strike defense is 52% career (worst in this matchup), and he fades late (R3 CARDIO RISK). As Landwehr's pressure slows in rounds 2–3, Swanson's selective counters find a tired, increasingly open target. The Quarantillo finish (R3 KO) shows Swanson can close the show when the moment arrives.

**#2 — Fight goes to decision, Swanson wins**
Probability: 24%
Why: Swanson's recent decision form (Dawodu, Kron Gracie) shows he can manage fights on the back foot for 3 rounds. If Landwehr's output is suppressed (as it was in his last 2 losses — 0.67 and 1.72 SLpM), Swanson's counter-striking accuracy advantage compounds across rounds. Judges tend to reward cleaner shots; Swanson at 54–68% accuracy vs. Landwehr at 36–39% in recent fights is a significant quality-of-work gap.

**#3 — Landwehr wins by KO/TKO, Round 1**
Probability: 18%
Why: Landwehr's only credible path is the opening round before his cardio fails and before Swanson times him. His best recent performance was 7.84 SLpM vs. Emmers (R1 finish). Swanson's chin is cracked (Chikadze R1, Martinez R2), and at 42 years old walking into the cage after a 479-day layoff, his reaction time is suspect. If Landwehr comes out with volume before Swanson finds his rhythm, the chin exposure is real.

**#4 — Fight goes to decision, Landwehr wins**
Probability: 16%

analysis_sections.betting:
**TIER: PLAY**
**Primary Pick: Cub Swanson — Confidence: 61%**

---

**Edge Breakdown:**

Line: Cub Swanson current −112
Implied probability: 100 / (112 + 100) = **52.8%**

Line: Nate Landwehr current −155
Implied probability: 155 / (155 + 100) = **60.8%**

Combined implied: 52.8% + 60.8% = **113.6%** (13.6% vig — aggressively juiced)

De-vig (Rule 11.2):
- True prob Swanson = 52.8 / 113.6 = **46.5%**
- True prob Landwehr = 60.8 / 113.6 = **53.5%**

**Assessed probability for Swanson: 61%**

*Derivation: Matrix baseline — FORWARD vs. BACKWARD structural edge: 48% Swanson baseline. Intangible adjustments: Swanson −25% total (age cliff capped −15%, layoff −10%); Landwehr −20% total (age cliff −5%, returning from KO loss −10%, R3 cardio risk modifier −5%). Net delta: Landwehr carries −20% vs. Swanson's −25%, meaning Swanson's intangible stack is worse. However, the ENDINGS analysis and style interaction shift assessed win prob to Swanson 61% / Landwehr 39% based on: (a) Swanson's counter-striking accuracy is measurably higher (54–68% recent vs. 36–39% Landwehr recent), (b) Landwehr's R3 CARDIO RISK fires in a way that exposes him to Swanson's most reliable weapon (late counters), (c) Landwehr's CHIN CRACKED status at 37 with back-to-back R3 KO losses amplifies Swanson's adjusted KO probability to ~28% for the finish scenarios, and (d) Swanson's decision-making has improved even as volume dropped. The line opened with Swanson as a mild favorite (+135/−135 equivalent range), moved to Landwehr −155 — a swing of roughly 19 implied probability points away from Swanson. This appears driven by public/recency bias on Landwehr's activity (269-day layoff vs. Swanson's 479) and Swanson's age, not by underlying stylistic merit.*

**Edge: 61% − 46.5% = +14.5%**

*Note: Edge above 12% triggers the Rule 11.3 re-check requirement. Re-examined: The edge is large because the line has moved aggressively toward Landwehr (from roughly even to −155 Landwehr) despite Landwehr having consecutive R3 KO losses and one of the worst strike defense rates in featherweight (52%). The market appears to be heavily discounting Swanson's age/layoff without fully repricing Landwehr's deteriorating recent performance. After re-check, the assessed edge is real but should be treated conservatively — cap confidence at 61% given both fighters are MEDIUM tier and the data for Landwehr's most recent fight (Charriere, Jul 2025) shows a fighter being finished in R3 for the second straight time. The edge estimate is retained but unit sizing is conservative.*

**Quarter-Kelly Stake:**
- Decimal odds for Swanson at −112: (100/112) + 1 = 1.893
- Kelly fraction: (0.145 × 1.893 − (1 − 0.61)) / (1.893 − 1) = (0.2745 − 0.39) / 0.893 = −0.1155 / 0.893

*Wait — Kelly is negative at this line. This means even at 61% assessed probability, the juice at −112 is eating most of the edge at this sizing. Let me recompute:*

- Kelly: (edge × decimal_odds − (1 − assessed_prob)) / (decimal_odds − 1)
- = (0.145 × 1.893 − 0.39) / (1.893 − 1)
- = (0.2745 − 0.39) / 0.893
- = −0.1155 / 0.893 = **−0.129**

*Negative Kelly = mathematically -EV at the current price of −112. Per Rule 11.4: "If quarter Kelly comes out negative, the bet is mathematically -EV — do not place it, regardless of how confident the read feels."*

**Reassessment required:**

Target line calculation — what's the worst price at which the bet still has +3% edge?

At +3% edge minimum, assessed prob = 61%, required: market true prob ≤ 58%.
De-vigged 58% on Swanson requires implied prob of approximately 58% × (1 + vig fraction).
At standard ~5% vig: implied ≈ 60.9% → American odds approximately **−156** would give implied 60.9% raw.
But the two-sided line must also set Landwehr's side. Realistically:
Swanson at **+100 or better** would give implied 50%, de-vigged against Landwehr's side would likely yield ~47–48% true prob, producing the necessary ~13% edge to clear +3% after Kelly.

More practically: **the value on Swanson exists only if you can get him at +100 or better** (positive money line). The current −112 is already too juiced for the Kelly math to work given the vig load. The market has moved from Swanson +135 (open) to −112 (current) — the opening line represented genuine value; the current line has partially closed it.

**Target line: Swanson +100 or better.**
Current market: Swanson −112. **Value is gone at this price.**

---

**Revised Recommendation:**

**TIER: NO BET (value eroded by line movement)**
**Pick: PASS**
**Confidence: 61%** (but Kelly math fires negative at −112)
**Edge at current price: mathematically -EV per quarter-Kelly**

The stat-based read favors Swanson — his counter-striking accuracy, Landwehr's R3 cardio collapse, and the CHIN CRACKED status on both fighters with the stylistic FORWARD/BACKWARD mismatch all point to Swanson. However, the line has moved from Swanson +135 (open) to −112 (current), eliminating the mathematical value. Anyone who caught the opening line at +135 had a strong bet; the closing number is overpriced for the edge available.

---

**Data Tiers:**
- Cub Swanson: **MEDIUM** (5+ UFC fights, full stats, but 479-day layoff makes data suspect)
- Nate Landwehr: **MEDIUM** (sufficient UFC fights, active, but consecutive KO losses raise data reliability questions)
- Active confidence cap: None formally (both MEDIUM = cap at 67%), but Kelly math overrides to NO BET regardless

**Market Read:**
- Open: Swanson −135 equivalent / Landwehr +115 equivalent (approximating from BFO data showing Swanson open +135 and Landwehr open +108 — this appears to be opening as Swanson slight underdog per the data provided)
- *Correction per data: Swanson open +135 → current −112 (shortened = became cheaper/more favorable for bettors, meaning money came IN on Swanson). Landwehr open +108 → current −155 (shortened = became favorite).*

*Wait — re-reading: "shortened" in the data for BOTH fighters. If Swanson moved from +135 to −112, that's the line MOVING TOWARD Swanson (shortening his odds from +135 to −112 means he went from underdog to slight favorite — that's sharp money on Swanson). If Landwehr moved from +108 to −155, that's the line moving toward Landwehr (he went from slight underdog to heavy favorite — sharp money on Landwehr). These movements contradict each other, which is impossible on a two-sided market.*

*Most likely interpretation: The signs in the data may be formatted as the direct cost to the bettor (all shortened = all got more expensive = public is betting both sides up in a pump). More likely, one side moved toward and one moved away. Given: "Reverse line movement: NO" — there is no RLM. Standard interpretation: Landwehr opened as slight favorite (−108 equivalent if Swanson was +135 open), and has moved to −155. Sharp and/or public money has flowed heavily to Landwehr. Swanson opened +135 and has moved to −112 — this does NOT make sense as "shortened toward Swanson" because that would make him nearly even money while Landwehr is −155 (impossible two-sided). Treating the data as: Swanson +135 open → +112 current (still underdog, line shortened meaning he got cheaper/better odds for bettors = money came off him). Landwehr −135 open → −155 current (heavier favorite, money went on Landwehr). This is consistent: public/money loading up on Landwehr, Swanson drifting slightly longer.*

- **Direction:** Money flowing to Landwehr. No RLM. Public-driven move toward the more active, younger (relatively) fighter and away from the 42-year-old with 479-day layoff. This is a classic narrative-driven public move — exactly the type Rule 10.10 identifies as fadeable.
- **RLM status:** NO RLM. Cannot confirm sharp money on Swanson.

**Hedge Verdict:**
- Best Swanson line: approximately −112 (current)
- Best Landwehr line: approximately −155 (current)
- Implied Swanson: 52.8% | Implied Landwehr: 60.8%
- Combined: 113.6%
- **Verdict: NO ARB** (combined implied 113.6% >> 100%). The 13.6% combined vig makes any hedge structure deeply -EV. Single-side value bet only — and at current prices, even that has been compromised.

**Props Worth Considering:**
If the fight goes to decision prop is available: the Section 8.4 decision probability estimate is ~51% (summing all decision outcomes: ~24% + ~16% = 40% in listed endings, but the adjusted KO probs leaving ~64% decision baseline before redistribution). If a "Fight goes to decision YES" prop is available at +130 or better (implied ~43%), it has potential value. De-vigged against "NO" at approximately −150 (implied 60%): combined 43% + 60% = 103% vig. True decision prob from our model: ~40%. Market true prob: approximately 40.8%. Edge: essentially 0%. **No prop value identified at standard lines.**

**Fade Alert:** None formally. Initial read was Swanson, final read remains Swanson, but the Kelly math overrides to NO BET at current price. The *directional read* is Swanson; the *betting recommendation* is PASS due to line movement having closed the value window.

---

**Summary:**
> *The stat-based read: Swanson at 61% assessed probability. The market's current implied (de-vigged): 46.5% for Swanson. That gap exists and is real — but the −112 price generates negative Kelly, meaning even a correct read doesn't produce positive expected value at this juice level. Anyone with access to Swanson at +100 or better should play it. At −112 current market: PASS.*

cub-swanson__nate-landwehr.raw_analysis:
<!--F1_PROFILE-->
## Cub Swanson -- Style & Profile

- **Data Tier: MEDIUM** (5+ UFC fights, active stats, but 479-day layoff makes recent data suspect per Rule 2.3/3.6). Archetype: **Power/Counter-Striker**. Tag: **BACKWARD** — Swanson wins by picking his spots, not initiating volume exchanges. Career SLpM 4.79 with 51% accuracy, but last-3 SLpM averages only 2.07/min — TRENDING DOWN sharply (-57% vs. career). Volume has collapsed.

- **CHIN CRACKED — flagged.** KO losses: Giga Chikadze R1 (1:03), Jonathan Martinez R2 (4:19). Two KO losses in last 3 years, one in R1, one in R2. Age 42 (5+ years past the 37-cliff for men in lower weight classes). Defensive decay partially confirmed: career SApM 4.00, but Swanson absorbed shots from a R1 KO (Chikadze) and R2 KO (Martinez). The Quarantillo KO win masks a fighter who is routinely getting hurt. Rule 6.2 criteria: ✅ 2+ KO losses in last 3 years, ✅ R1 KO loss (Chikadze), ✅ Age 42 (cliff condition).

- **Age cliff penalty fires hard.** At 42 in the featherweight division (cliff = 35), that's 7 years past cliff. Rule 9.7: −5% per year, capped at −15%. Full cap applied. His win over Quarantillo was real (R3 KO, 68.4% accuracy in that fight — career-high accuracy), but at 42 with 479 days off, that performance is the outlier, not the baseline.

- **Layoff: 479 days.** Rule 9.2: 365–540 days = −10% penalty AND career stats treated as suspect. Swanson's last fight was December 2024; this is a serious comeback timeline at age 42. No reported injury context available to adjust the penalty.

- **Striking accuracy trending interestingly:** last-3 Str. Acc. averages (68.4%, 64.5%, 54.8%) are all ABOVE career 51% — suggesting he's throwing less (SLpM collapsed) but landing at higher accuracy when he does throw. Classic counter-striker aging pattern: he's become more selective, not more effective. This works until he gets hit by someone who punishes hesitancy.

- **Grappling is effectively non-existent in recent fights.** Zero takedown attempts in all three last-3 fights. Career TD avg 1.01/15min is hollow — he hasn't used the grappling dimension in 18+ months of documented fights.
<!--END-->

<!--F2_PROFILE-->
## Nate Landwehr -- Style & Profile

- **Data Tier: MEDIUM** (sufficient UFC fights, 269-day layoff within acceptable range, but recent fight data raises serious concerns). Archetype: **Volume Striker / Brawler**. Tag: **FORWARD** — Landwehr initiates, walks forward, and seeks exchanges. Career SLpM 5.63 places him firmly in the volume striker tier, but critically he absorbs 5.83/min — one of the worst SApM rates at featherweight.

- **CHIN CRACKED — flagged.** Two consecutive KO losses: Dooho Choi R3 (Dec 2024) and Morgan Charriere R3 (Jul 2025). Both in Round 3 — this is technically a **cardio-related KO pattern** (Rule 6.4: late stoppages = cardio problem), BUT the back-to-back nature combined with his career-high SApM (5.83) and strike defense trending: career Str. Def. 52% (already low) with last-3 accuracy collapsing to 39.1% and 36.0% — these are deterioration signals. Rule 6.2 criteria: ✅ 2+ KO losses in last 3 years, ✅ SApM career 5.83 (defensive decay signal), ✅ Age 37 (at the cliff for featherweight men). CHIN CRACKED confirmed with cardio overlay.

- **Striking stats in free fall.** Last-3 SLpM: 7.84 (vs. Emmers, WIN), 0.67 (vs. Choi, LOSS), 1.72 (vs. Charriere, LOSS). The Emmers outlier flatters the average. In his two most recent fights (both losses), he landed 0.67 and 1.72/min against a career average of 5.63 — TRENDING DOWN severely in meaningful fights. Str. Acc.: 39.1% and 36.0% in last two — well below career 45%. The "volume striker" identity is eroding in real competition.

- **R3 CARDIO RISK — flagged.** Both recent KO losses came in Round 3. Pattern: Landwehr starts fights forward and active (7.84 vs. Emmers in a short fight), but fades badly as fights progress. Career high SApM suggests he takes punishment early and pays for it late. Rule 7.1: 2+ R3+ losses in last 5 fights = R3 CARDIO RISK explicitly fired.

- **Grappling: low volume but functional.** Career Sub. Avg. 0.8/15min with 2 submission wins in last 10 fights (Klein R3, Lingo R2). TD Def. 71% is respectable. He won't be a grappling force but can threaten from scrambles if the fight goes to the mat. However: zero takedown attempts in each of the last 3 fights — the grappling dimension has also gone dormant.

- **Age cliff: 37, exactly at the featherweight cliff.** Rule 9.7: −5% penalty at cliff initiation. Combined with R3 CARDIO RISK and CHIN CRACKED, this is a fighter whose physical tools are declining on multiple axes simultaneously.
<!--END-->

<!--HEAD2HEAD-->
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

This matchup pits two CHIN CRACKED, aging featherweights against each other in what the matrix identifies as a **FORWARD (Landwehr) vs. BACKWARD (Swanson)** fight. Per Rule 4.4: the FORWARD fighter (Landwehr) controls cage and output, while the BACKWARD fighter (Swanson) wins exchanges. The structural question becomes: which fighter can execute their identity for more than one round at age 42 and 37, respectively, when both have documented deterioration? The matrix says the BACKWARD counter-striker wins the *exchanges* — but Swanson hasn't consistently executed counter-striking in recent fights. His last-3 SLpM (2.07 average) is barely above Landwehr's collapsed recent numbers (1.72 and 0.67). Neither fighter is functioning anywhere near their career archetype identity.

The critical exchange is simple: Landwehr's opening-round pressure vs. Swanson's counter-striking windows. Landwehr generated 7.84 SLpM against Emmers in a short finish — when he's on, he's on. Against Swanson's 59% career strike defense (already mediocre) and CHIN CRACKED status at 42, Landwehr's pressure *should* generate early openings. However, Landwehr's own defense is catastrophic — 52% career strike defense, and Swanson's counter-striking accuracy has paradoxically *improved* in recent fights (54–68% accuracy) even as volume collapsed. A single clean counter from Swanson — who has 3 KO wins in last 10 fights — could end matters. The key variable is **whether Landwehr can sustain pressure without fading to a point where Swanson's counters find him open**. Given Landwehr's R3 CARDIO RISK and his last two fights ending in R3 stoppages, the answer leans toward "probably not for a full 3 rounds."

Net intangible delta favors **Swanson slightly, perversely**: Swanson's 479-day layoff (−10%) and age cliff (−15%, capped) are brutal, but Landwehr's R3 CARDIO RISK, consecutive KO losses (Rule 6.3: −10% for returning from KO loss), age cliff (−5%), and his own chin deterioration produce a similar or worse intangible stack. Swanson: −25% total (layoff −10%, age cliff capped −15%). Landwehr: −20% estimated (age −5%, returning from KO loss −10%, R3 cardio risk modifier). Both fighters are operating at significantly reduced win probability from their "peak" archetypes — the fight becomes a question of who deteriorates *less*, not who performs best.

**Flip conditions (Rule 15.1):** If Landwehr's R3 losses were primarily due to opponent-specific factors (e.g., Choi's power specifically, Charriere's reach advantage) rather than systemic cardio collapse, downgrade the cardio risk and lean harder to Landwehr. If Swanson's 479-day layoff included a disclosed injury, push to NO BET. If scheduled rounds are 5 (not 3), switch to heavy Swanson fade — his chin and age make 5 rounds untenable.
<!--END-->

<!--ENDINGS-->
## 3-5 Most Likely Fight Endings (Ranked by Probability)

**Section 8 math baseline:**
- Swanson KO base rate: 3 KOs in last 10 = 30% career-recent rate. Adjusted: 0.30 × (1 − 0.52 Landwehr Str. Def.) × 1.4 (Landwehr CHIN CRACKED) × 1.0 = 0.30 × 0.48 × 1.4 = **~20% adjusted KO prob for Swanson**
- Landwehr KO base rate: 1 KO in last 10 = 10% (his wins are submissions and decisions recently). Career KO % is misleadingly low in last 10. Using 25% career rate adjusted: 0.25 × (1 − 0.59 Swanson Str. Def.) × 1.4 (Swanson CHIN CRACKED) × 1.0 = 0.25 × 0.41 × 1.4 = **~14% adjusted KO prob for Landwehr**
- Sub prob both fighters: near zero given 0 TD attempts in each fighter's last 3 fights.
- Decision prob: 1 − 0.20 − 0.14 − ~0.02 (subs both) ≈ **~64% decision probability baseline**. This feels high given both fighters' finish history, so we'll redistribute some to rounds-specific finishes.

**#1 — Cub Swanson wins by KO/TKO, Round 2 or 3**
Probability: 28%
Why: Swanson's counter-striking accuracy has been 54–68% in recent fights, the highest of his career — he's landing cleaner than ever when he throws. Landwehr's chin is cracked after back-to-back R3 losses, his strike defense is 52% career (worst in this matchup), and he fades late (R3 CARDIO RISK). As Landwehr's pressure slows in rounds 2–3, Swanson's selective counters find a tired, increasingly open target. The Quarantillo finish (R3 KO) shows Swanson can close the show when the moment arrives.

**#2 — Fight goes to decision, Swanson wins**
Probability: 24%
Why: Swanson's recent decision form (Dawodu, Kron Gracie) shows he can manage fights on the back foot for 3 rounds. If Landwehr's output is suppressed (as it was in his last 2 losses — 0.67 and 1.72 SLpM), Swanson's counter-striking accuracy advantage compounds across rounds. Judges tend to reward cleaner shots; Swanson at 54–68% accuracy vs. Landwehr at 36–39% in recent fights is a significant quality-of-work gap.

**#3 — Landwehr wins by KO/TKO, Round 1**
Probability: 18%
Why: Landwehr's only credible path is the opening round before his cardio fails and before Swanson times him. His best recent performance was 7.84 SLpM vs. Emmers (R1 finish). Swanson's chin is cracked (Chikadze R1, Martinez R2), and at 42 years old walking into the cage after a 479-day layoff, his reaction time is suspect. If Landwehr comes out with volume before Swanson finds his rhythm, the chin exposure is real.

*Probabilities sum: 28 + 24 + 18 + 16 + 9 = 95% (5% residual for unlisted outcomes: draws, no-contests, submission)*
